speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The same stuff that we were talking about the first time I was here because that was right on the money, that kind of stuff, just the interesting kinds of things about cooking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: First a little background, I was wondering about, you had said that your father was a cook and also, right?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Yeah, that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So how was it that he came to come out here from South Dakota?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, he came out here from Dakota, I think it was about 1907.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: And I went all through school here in Spokane. And that is, I went to the old Hilliard High School over here in Hilliard. And my dad, he worked in different restaurants and bakeries here in Spokane for a number of years. And then at different times he had restaurants of his own here. I don't know, I guess over the years he probably had two or three different restaurants here in town. I kind of grew up in the food business, more or less, like that. And naturally, when I started to work, I gravitated to the kitchen. And that's what I followed nearly all my life, in the food industry one way and another.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: So I, when I was in high school, that is during the summer vacations, I would hire out a lot of people out into the lumber camps, you know, and work during the summer. And maybe a month or so, maybe after school started even, to get money. Of course, we didn't get a lot of money those days, but it went quite a ways, what we did get. So that's how I started into the camps. I would go out in the summer and work in the kitchen. And then come back and, well after I graduated from high school, I went up near Bovill to work for the potlatch forest there. And I stayed there for quite a number of years, until nineteen. I think it was 1944, my wife and I moved to Spokane.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell me that you remembered standing up on the box and washing dishes when you were just a kid?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Oh yes, I can remember that very well, because my dad had restaurants here while I was a pretty young fellow. And of course, to help out, I would do what I could around the kitchen. Washing dishes was naturally the easiest thing that I could do. And sometimes, we just had those big old wooden sinks in those days. There was no automatic dishwashers or anything like that. And we'd wash the dishes in one sink in the soapy water, and then we'd rinse them in the other sink in the clear water, and then stack them up on what they call a draining board and let them dry off up there, and then wipe them with a towel. And that was what I did, you know, sometimes before school and after school. Got help out on the budget. And I'd have a box there, I was a pretty little fellow, and I'd have a box to stand on so I could handle things a little better.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: When you got... I'm not talking too much now. No, no, you're not. I just want to tell you what I... You want to know. Yeah. I don't mean to give too much detail. No, I like that the detail is the most interesting. I don't want to project myself into this thing anymore.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: But... So then after I grew a little older, I branched out all myself, and I got into the kitchen with these different cooks, you know, that had been at that most all their lives, and I picked up their methods and their secrets, they called them, making different things, and I worked with some very good cooks up there, very excellent cooks they were. And there was quite a number of them that were very excellent cooks. I can look back now and realize that I worked with one man there, that his name was Herman Dutzahl. And he was, I believe, that Herman, I think, in my personal opinion, I think he was the best all -around cook that I ever saw. And he could make anything and everything and make it good. And he, when I went up there to work for him, he was getting to be quite aged then, and

speaker: Albert Justice
words: he wouldn't work where they had the large crew of men like we used to have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd wish it wasn't, you want to take it out of the oven? And that he didn't used to work for large crews, you say?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: No, he wouldn't. That is, when I knew him, he was getting, like I say, a little bit aged for a heavy, big crew, and he wouldn't work in a camp where there was over, say, 50 men or so.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: But for that reason, they used to, they put him on what they called the steel gang. That was a traveling car camp that all they did was lay steel and take up steel and all like that, make new track and put it wherever they wanted. And that railroad, well, they called the steel gang. They had about 45, 50 men, I imagine, in that crew. And he was on that steel gang for quite a number of years. And I worked with him there for, oh, I guess I was with Harmon there probably for a year when there. And he taught me a lot of things because he was getting, like I say, a little bit. And he had a chair there in one corner of the kitchen, and he would sit in that chair, and now he'd tell me what to do. Of course, I was washing dishes there and doing some other work. Well, I'd get my work through, you know. So then he'd say, well, kid, come on, and I'll show you how to do this and do that. He'd show me how to mix the bread and what to put in it and so forth like that, and the pie crust and all, how to make all those different things, cookies and cakes and all like that. And he'd sit over there and tell me what to, and after a while, you know, I got so I knew just about how to do it myself, and I went right ahead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he patient with you?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah, he was very, yeah, he was very, Harmon was a very nice fellow. We got along just fine. And, but like I say, I think Harmon was really, of all the different cooks I knew up there, I think Harmon was, and I think that, as Ralph Hanson, if you talk to him, I think he'll tell you, I think he knew Harmon, too, and of course, Ralph didn't come into the camps until a little later, but I think he'll tell you, too, that Harmon was a very excellent cook.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember still some of the tricks that he taught you that stayed with you and became the way you cooked?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, I'll tell you, actually, there are no tricks. There really isn't, I can't think of any tricks, actually. It's the proper ingredients in anything that you're cooking, I think, is more important than any kind of tricks. I don't know of any, really any tricks, what you might call. The main trick, I think, is when you're working, like preparing food for a big group that way, is to get so you can do these things and do them fast. Get so you can do things fast. I think that's the main trick. And as far as being any tricks, actually, I can't, you know, and I like rolling out a pie crust or something like that. You get so you can do that fast if you persevere and practice. And that's the main trick is to get so you can do it fast because when you're making 50, 60 pies every morning, you can't be fooling around there till noon rolling out those pie crusts. And that's the thing, and I think the main trick in any kind of cookery is the right proportion of ingredients and learn to do it so that you don't have to look at the recipe every time. You got those things in your head. You know, like I say, pie crusts and cakes and cookies and donuts and all those different things that we made, you have them in your head. Once in a great while you might look at the recipe just for... But when you got that amount of food to prepare, and you had to have it on time too, I'll tell you what I mean, the meals, you have to learn to do these things and do them fast, you know, like we used to roll out. And Ralph will tell you the same thing. He used to roll the pie a minute there, you know. And of course we'd have all our filling made the night before, like if we're going to make a two double -faced pie, we'd have all the filling made the night before and the pie crust and everything ready the night before, you see. Well, when Ralph would start, he used to, when we had the victory, he would make the pie in the morning and he would roll the pie a minute right along there. And what he did, he put the bottom crust in and he filled it and put the top crust on and sealed it and do that in a minute. And he'd do that for maybe an hour. He'd have 60 pies out there. Well, like I say, you couldn't be fooling around there until noon getting those pies out. So in order to get fast with things like that, you'd have to have breakfast. Where did the recipes, the recipes come from that he used and that you... Well, we got them from different cookbooks and different fellas and all like Herman. I got quite a number of recipes from Herman because I knew they were all right, you know. And I got recipes from other different ones like Red Watson here, you saw him there. And well, you just kind of gather and you go along, you see. You go into some camp and the cookie got loosey. And that's a good looking cookie and that's an awful good looking doughnut or something like that. Well, it's a recipe. Well, he'd give you the recipe for it, see. Well, you try them out and if you like them, well, they just kind of accumulate and gather as time goes on. Sometimes you can buy a good cookbook and use some of those. But the recipes, most of them, the way he went by were ones that had been tried out and that's the way we come by most of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have baking powder when you first came into camp?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: No, not when I first came into camp. But he was still doing sour dough and he even made cakes with sour dough, you know. No baking powder, no cream of tartar, and no, well, we didn't have baking powder for quite a long while after I, but after we got baking powder, that simplified our baking to a great extent because it's much quicker and much handier.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: And we got better results from it, I think, after we kind of learned how to use it. But no, no, there was just a lot of things we didn't have, but gradually things like that started to come into camp. The food kept getting better and better all the time, I think. But they always had the abundance of good substantial helpful food. But it was a very limited variety. There was plenty of quantity, and the quality was all right, too, but it was very, we didn't have fresh vegetables very much, and we had potatoes and things like that, but canned fruit and canned vegetables, and they were nonexistent in the camps for quite a few years when I first got in there. Dried apples and dried peaches and dried prunes and dried apricots. Of course, then the dried fruit was reasonably cheap compared to what it is now. Dried fruit now is almost out of sight, but we had plenty of that, and dried apples and lots of dried prunes and raisins, we had that. We used to make our pies out of, well, we'd make our pies out of dried apples and dried apricots, and sometimes we'd make, well, we didn't have eggs, you see. We couldn't make custard pies or pumpkin pies or anything like that because we didn't have any eggs there for a long time to speak of. Once in a while we'd get a case of eggs. Well, it went very sparingly on those eggs, but we were, you might say, handicapped in some ways to give the men a variety because we just didn't have it. Eggs, you know, that's quite a versatile thing to cook with. You can do many things with eggs, but custard puddings and all like that, you know, you can't make those without eggs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would the breakfast, let's go through a couple of different meals, what would the breakfast be like in the early days? What would they have for breakfast?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, usually in the early days one of the standard dishes for breakfast was baked beans. We'd have those every morning, baked beans and hotcakes.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: And usually, nearly well every morning there was some kind of hot cereal, oatmeal usually. That would be about, sometimes we'd have what they used to call farina. Those days they call it cream of wheat now, but we used to get farina in big ten -pound bags and then we'd have farina and oatmeal and then we didn't have ham or bacon, but we had salt pork. And quite often we'd get the salt pork for breakfast. But they baked beans and fried potatoes, nearly always fried potatoes, and hot cereal, and lots of bread, usually hotcakes, lots of coffee.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Milk was another thing that was, well it was kind of scarce, that is canned milk. We used to take the canned milk and reduce it with water, you know, about half and half. And then we'd put that in the pitcher, along on the table, you know, for their moisture.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: But we didn't have milk enough, that is canned milk, to set the can on the table for their coffee. If they wanted milk in their coffee, like say they'd cream in their coffee, they'd put that in out of that pitcher. Well that was half and half, what they used to call sixteen to one. That's what the lumberjacks used to call it, sixteen to one, but it was about half and half. And then we would put these pitchers along on the table and they'd put that on their moisture and put it in their coffee if they wanted, but it was not very rich or anything like coffee cream.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was hotcakes as popular as they say nowadays?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: No, they say hotcakes are real popular, the lumberjacks always ate hotcakes. Oh yeah, hotcakes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that sort of a staple, a big thing for breakfast?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Oh yeah, hotcakes you bet. Well, in the early times, hotcakes wasn't quite so, and then bread, there was lots of bread on the table and things like that. But in the early, they really didn't have the facilities to bake hotcakes because they didn't have a big, what we call a big griddle. But all the long ways back, hotcakes were just a must. You had hotcakes every morning, regardless of what other things you had, hotcakes. And of course we always had toast, buttered toast and hotcakes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you start there in the morning to get this breakfast on the table for the men? When did the crew get going?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, we'd usually have breakfast at six o 'clock. That was about the standard time for breakfast. Lunch or dinner, we called it, would be about eleven thirty.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: And then evening meal, I think usually was five thirty. But way back before 1917, you see, they were working ten hours in the woods then. Well, that necessitated the meals being a little farther apart. You see, that is like supper would be a little later, maybe six thirty. And dinner and lunch would be probably twelve, you see. Well, they were working ten hours in the woods then. Well, after they got the eight hour day, of course that allowed us to shorten up the periods in between the meals a little bit. Like we'd have breakfast at six o 'clock and then lunch at about eleven thirty. Well, then evening meal about five thirty. So that shortened up our day a little bit, all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did your day start?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, when I had a big crew, now you'd say maybe two hundred and fifty, three hundred men. Of course, I was responsible for... I would be in the kitchen at three o 'clock in the morning, see, to get... You didn't only have to get breakfast, but you had to start lunch and also supper, you see, because those things had to be coming. You couldn't wait till supper time to get supper, or wait till lunch time to get lunch. And a lot of other things too. Like Ralph, he used to get up about four. Well, I guess the first thing Ralph would do would be to mix a batch of bread. That was about his first thing in the morning. Well, he'd be in there about four o 'clock. Well, then after he got... We used to have a great big wash tub, one of the biggest we could get, and he'd mix that by hand. We had a little little table about like this. And he'd set that wash tub on there and then he'd put his ingredients in. And the flour bin was right alongside of him there, and he'd mix that by hand. And then when he got his bread mixed, then he'd jump onto these pies. And he'd have those pies all made before the breakfast bell rang. We'd ring two bells every morning. One bell was to get them up, that is to, you know, like the... We used to call that the first bell. And then there was the second bell. The first bell was to give them notice down in the bunkhouse to get up to breakfast. And of course, a good many of them were up before that, but we'd give them half an hour, see, between the first bell and the second bell. We'd give them 30 minutes. Well, we'd ring that first bell right on the top there about 5 .30. Well, then the breakfast bell would be six o 'clock, and we'd ring that and that's coming up. But they'd be standing out there, maybe a hundred of them waiting to get in there. And... We'd have one of those big triangles, you know. And there was always one fella's duty in the kitchen to watch that clock and ring that first bell. Usually the dishwasher would... That was his... He was assigned to ring the first bell, and I had to watch that myself because sometimes they'd forget, you know. And I was responsible for everything that went on in there. And if anything was wrong, I was the one that had the answer for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about second meal? Dinner? Lunch? What was usually served at that in the early days?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, of course in the early days we had beef and pork, and that was about it. Beef and pork. And usually in the early days we didn't get the very choice of beef. It would be mostly front quarters. And of course front quarter is not really choice, like I mean for steaks and things like that. So that gave us quite a bit of roast beef and beef stew, and we had to grind our own hamburger. And you would have hamburger and stew and roast beef, and then you could have boiled beef and all things like that. And of course that was... For lunch we would have, well, just about, you know, vegetable, potatoes, and meat, and maybe beef stew, or maybe roast beef, or braised beef, or many different kinds of... You know, fix the beef in many different ways. We would have potatoes and gravy and vegetable and always the dessert of some kind. Pie usually, and pudding, or lots of bread. We always had cookies and donuts and so forth like that, and that all went on the table for lunch. It's the same with...

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Evening meal was usually... You know, it used to be, and of course it was right up until quite late, they used to... An awful lot of them would carry a lunch, you know, because they had so far to go. So they would take a lunch in a bucket, you know.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: And that was quite a problem. That was really a problem at lunch business, and we tried it in several different methods how to pack these lunches. And one time we had a man, that's all he did, pack lunches at night, see, for these men the next morning. He'd pack these lunches at night. He'd make the sandwiches and put in whatever else went in the lunch, like a piece of pie or cookies or whatever went in there, an orange or whatever. And that was his job. He'd slice the lunch meat and make the sandwiches and pack the lunch and set them all along. We had a big long table and he'd set these lunches all along that table so that these fellows, when they got up from the breakfast table, they could pick up their lunch. Well that didn't prove quite satisfactory either. There was some dissatisfaction about that because people have many different preferences in what they like to eat. I think more than any other one thing. People are fussy. Some people are more fussy than others. Hi Billy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So some people weren't satisfied with whatever they got?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well you couldn't satisfy, like say if you had 300 men, you couldn't satisfy all those fellows no matter what you put in that bucket. We'd try to make these sandwiches. Of course we'd put butter on them. But the thing is when you're packing that many lunches you've got to pretty much pack them all the same. You can't hardly personalize lunches, that is to pack an individual lunch for each individual man just what he wants. That would be too much of a job. Maybe not like, I can't eat any butter, don't put any butter on my bread. Another one would say, I can't eat boiled eggs or mayonnaise on my lettuce or something like that. Well you just couldn't go ahead and pack what each man wanted separately.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So how did you deal with that?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well you just couldn't deal with it. Like I say there was some dissatisfaction because, well this guy would say I'm on a diet, I can't eat any, don't give me any meat or don't give me any, not like maybe you'd have roast pork sandwich. Oh he'd say I can't eat pork, I've got to have, many things come up like that, different ones say well I don't like so and so. And don't put any, we'd have maybe two or three different kinds of bread, rye bread, whole wheat bread, white bread. One guy'd say I can't eat whole wheat bread. Another guy says I can't eat rye bread, I can't eat, it'd make me sick. Well, things like that, it gets to be quite, so the only thing you can do is just say well, if you can't eat whole wheat bread it's just too bad, you just have to.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: And, but later on, we tried another system. Let each man pack his own lunch. So what we did then, we had a separate room that they fixed up with a long table see. And we'd set all the food out on that long table, like the bread would be sliced and the butter was there and the meat would be sliced and all the different things that went in that lunch would be there, like pickles and everything on that table see. And they could go along that table and make their own sandwiches. They could put in whatever they wanted. If they wanted a piece of pie, all right. If they wanted a doughnut or a cookie or whatever they wanted, peanut butter sandwich or anything was all there. And then down there, there was a big pot of coffee and sugar and cream and all like that. They could fill their own, they had vacuum bottles. The company, when they started that, the company furnished the lunch pail see. And each lunch pail had a vacuum bottle in it for coffee or whatever they wanted to put in. So they, each man got a lunch pail and a complete kit there to pack his lunch. So then they would come before breakfast you see and pack their lunch. Long about, well, sometime shortly after five o 'clock, even before the first bell, you'd see some of those guys coming in. We'd have that all set out there see, early in the morning. And they'd start packing their lunch. Well, you could pack just what you wanted.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Most fellas would really take more than they would eat, you know. I don't say everyone, but some of them would take more than they could eat. And because the attitude was, among a good many of those fellas, that they were paying too much for their board. And they would figure, oh well, I'm paying for this anyway and if I throw away a cookie or a sandwich or something, I'm paying for it anyhow. But actually, I guess it was really more satisfactory for the men when they packed their own lunch. Because they could take as much as they wanted or whatever they wanted. And if they didn't have what they wanted, it was their own fault. Because there was practically everything on that table. There'd probably be five or six kinds of sandwich meat, you know. Lunch meat and roast beef, roast pork, all this ham, all the ham they wanted. And fried eggs. I'd always fry a bunch of eggs for their sandwiches. I'd fry them in the morning so they'd be warm if they wanted an egg sandwich. And there was everything. Jelly, peanut butter, and all those kinds of things there on the table.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was supper a bigger meal than lunch in the kitchen?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, yes, it was. Because, well, of course everybody was in for supper. Like these fellas that took a cold lunch. Well, when you pack a cold lunch, I don't care what that is, it's not like a hot meal. And naturally when those fellas came in at night, some of them didn't take much lunch. Some of them would just take a little paper sack. Well, naturally when they came in, especially in the wintertime, after eating a cold lunch out there, maybe just grab a snack and hurry. Well, they were pretty hungry, you know. So the supper meal, the evening meal, was always the big meal because everybody was in for supper and they were pretty hungry. We always tried to give them, really, the biggest, most generous meal at suppertime because everybody was, like I say, they were hungry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it pretty similar to the dinner for the people that were in camp for dinner time?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Oh, yeah, there'd be quite a few in camp for dinner, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it the same type of food that you served at lunch?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, you know, like, say maybe there'd be a hundred men in for lunch in camp for the noon meal. Well, we'd give them a regular lunch, like hot, maybe roast beef or roast pork, beef stew and all those things, potatoes, gravy and everything like that. But, well, what I mean more or less, like a big meal, like for the evening meal, what I mean, it was the whole crew was in. It was a big meal, you see, of course. Of course, for breakfast they was all in too, but breakfast is not considered quite as full a meal as like supper, you know, because, oh yeah, for lunch we'd give them a full meal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But what would supper be? What would you serve them at supper for the big meal of the day?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, just for, maybe for instance, we'd say, well, we'll say maybe pork chops. This is just a sample. Well, maybe it would be pork chops and maybe baked potatoes. And there's always a second meat of some kind, like, well, maybe we'd have pork chops, maybe we'd give them a southern hash or something like that, see, to go with a pork chop. That is just for, some people didn't like pork chops, you know, maybe they'd be, well, they had to have some other kind of a meat on there for, well, maybe we'd say we'd have pork chops and southern hash, maybe baked potatoes and we'd have probably creamed gravy. And with those pork chops we'd usually have applesauce, you see. And then, of course, we'd have vegetables. There's always two hot vegetables besides potatoes. Maybe it would be string beans and always two hot vegetables besides the potatoes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would this be in the early days as well?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, of course, in the early days we didn't have quite the variety that we had later on. But, of course, in the early days we'd had, we didn't have the canned vegetables, like I say, but we'd have lots of beef and, but mostly we didn't have steaks and things like that very much in the early days because we didn't get the right kind of meat to get the steaks from. But it would be mostly roast beef and beef stew and hamburgers and things like that in the early days, but there was lots of it and it was usually well cooked and all. But... So supper was really the big... When we got up in the morning we weren't only getting breakfast, we were starting lunch and supper too because a lot of that stuff had to be, you know, brought up quite a while beforehand. You have to figure the room you have on the stove and your oven room and how long it takes to do these things and it takes... You cut a little planning ahead because you've got to keep things coming one after the other there. I think it would take a lot of planning, a lot of knowledge. It did take a lot of planning because I had to do all the ordering, you see, all the food and I had to keep a certain supply on hand but not too much. Because one of our big problems in the early days was there was no refrigeration, no place to keep anything, you see. Like, well, like our meat, we didn't have no place to, you know, only just outside in a little screened in shack out there. And leftovers, there was no refrigeration, no place to... It was quite a problem to keep enough things on hand and yet not get too much because... And then in the early days, you know, you'd probably only get supplies once a week. Maybe in the wintertime you wouldn't get them that often. And if there's a way back in the woods and they had to go in after things with a sleigh, maybe it only went in once or twice a week, well, your supplies come once or twice a week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You put in the order and then Potlatch got the supplies?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Yeah, I put in the order and then they... That went into the warehouse there and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did they usually come from, the supplies?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, they'd buy them from the wholesale houses around, different places, you know, and... A lot of it here in Spokane from these wholesale groceries and meat packers and the like of that. They'd buy their meat. They used to buy a lot of meat at Lewiston, I know, from the meat packer down there.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: But we didn't, in the early days, we didn't get the best of meat, you know, that is... But there was always lots of it and it was like the front quarters were not... They're not choice beef, that is, for steaks and things, but... Plenty of roast beef and stews and hamburgers and braised beefs and all like that, but... I'd like to ask you a little bit about... I want to come back to the cooking, but I'd like to know when you first came out in the woods what the conditions were like when you were very first... You were there, you were saying before that...

speaker: Albert Justice
words: When I first went into the woods?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, I'll tell you. Actually, the conditions were... I can't think of anything that won't be there, were terrible. That is, for the outside man, what I mean the men working in the woods. But the conditions were very bad. That's all I can say is that... Because I can think back and... But like I say, I was always in the kitchen and our conditions were a little better because we had a little more opportunity, a little better opportunity to... Like I say, wash our clothes and things like that, you know. But when you're outside there with that crew of men, maybe there'd be a hundred men in one big long bunkhouse, you see. A big stove in the middle and bunks down each side. Well, no place to wash your clothes. If you can just imagine if you didn't have any place to wash your clothes and no place to take a bath. No place to take a bath.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, you come in, especially in the wintertime, and your feet are, you know, you get your feet wet and maybe your shirt is wet and your pants are wet. I used to wear what they call tin pants. And they were kind of a khaki pants, but they had been dipped in paraffin, see. And they were stiff. They'd almost stand alone on the floor. They called them tin pants because they were almost like tin when you had them on. But they all wore tin pants there in the wet weather, see. Well, you're bound to get wet no matter. They'd get here where they broke the knees, you see, that paraffin would crack, see. And that would get wet. Well, anyway, they'd come in at night and their clothes were wet. Well, they got to dry them out some way for the next morning. Well, they'd have two big long clothes lines the full length of this bunkhouse up above that stove, you see. Well, that's the only place they had to dry their socks and their shirts or anything. Underwear. Well, everybody'd hang their stuff on these lines all the way down this... There'd be socks and shirts and underwear hanging all over that bunkhouse all night long. Well, you can imagine what kind of an atmosphere, that's what I mean, odor, that'd be, from all those drying clothes there. And then they'd build a fire in that stove, of course. And, of course, the men on each side of the stove, close to the stove along at night, in the wintertime especially, they'd have a fire all night. Well, those fellas along close to the stove, they'd be roasting, you know. Well, these guys down at the end here, they'd be freezing to death, see. Well, that was part of the condition, that was one of the... There was nowhere to wash, what I mean, there was just outside of a wash basin they had in the bunkhouse, you know. They'd have a basin there and they'd wash there and there were a fish and hands in the morning. But, they'd wash your clothes, take a bath, there was no facilities to do that with. And, that's like I say, that led to, well, vermin, you know what I mean. And then the bedding, sleeping conditions was very bad because all of the bunks were just like... Say, they had four 1x12 boards laid in a frame like this, see. And then there was a side on it about 4 or 5 inches high, see. Well, if you wanted a mattress, you went out to the barn, they had horses, you went out to the barn and you got an armload of hay or straw and threw in the bottom. Just say, this is your bunk, see. You threw this in the bottom, and it was hay. And then you threw a blanket over that, that was your mattress. Well, all right, say you were sleeping here in this bed, you got two or three blankets there and you were sleeping there by yourself. Well, okay. Well, of course the men are coming and going all the time, some are quitting and some are coming. Well, here comes a strange guy in for a job. Well, I put him to work, see. Comes in here tonight, maybe this afternoon, and I can't for him and he puts him to work. Well, you got to have a place to sleep. Well, here you're alone in this bed, see. That's what they call a double bed, what I mean widely. Well, he got to have a place to sleep, so he crawls in there with you. He's got two or three blankets, you don't know who he is, you never saw him before, maybe he's a, some, you know, maybe he's clean, maybe he is, you don't know. But the rule is that you've got to get him half of that bed because that's the rule of the, well, here's some strange guy you never saw before, maybe he's, maybe he's lousy, maybe he's anything. He's in there in bed with you. You see, that's bad.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: I think that's very bad, don't you?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: It's not terrible, I mean. Well, it is terrible when you come to think about it. Because anybody coming along, like, stranger, you never saw him before, maybe he's, maybe he's alright, maybe he's, but maybe he isn't either, you see.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Now, what are you going to do?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, you've got to give that guy half of that bunk because that is the way that was set up. And I think that was one of the very worst things that, well, maybe this guy's blankets, lousy, see. Nine times out of ten they were. Because you don't know who he's been sleeping with before and all over the country, they go from camp to camp, you know, there was hundreds of camps some days and you could maybe walk from one camp to another in maybe an hour or so down the trail.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it, it was real, was it hard to get rid of the lice?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, you couldn't get rid of them, it was impossible.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: How are you going to get rid of them?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: There's no way to wash your clothes, no way to take a bath.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: How are you going to wash those blankets?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: And even if you did wash them, it wouldn't kill those eggs. Those, those, those lice are in those blankets. They're not only in the blankets, they're in that, they're down in that straw. They're in these little, you know, these boards that just nail together. They're down in the cracks, so this, you can get the lice, pretty much. That is a lot of them, but you can't get those eggs. They're down in under, in here in these sheets. And that straw would probably be alive with them, see.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, how are you going to get rid of them?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: You got no place to wash. No washroom. No bathhouse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that, that those conditions were the main reason why they had the, just the troubles in the camps?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Yeah, that's right. The conditions in the camps were what brought about the strike of 1917, of the IWWs. And I think they were fully justified in almost whatever they did. I don't believe in destroying property, burning timber, anything like that. But I think that the Wobblies, the IWWs were fully justified to go on strike because the conditions were, you can see they were, this is not exaggeration, this is absolute fact, I've seen it all. And the conditions were practically intolerable, you know, but it was the way that the lumber camps had started out back east in Minnesota and back in there many, many years ago. That's the way it was, and those conditions just naturally followed through out here, see. But I give the IWWs credit more than any other organization for cleaning up not only the lumber camps but the mines as well. The mines were pretty bad too, but I never worked in the mines, but even at that I know they were, they were pretty bad, practically as bad as that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the IWW did do the things, kinds of things that were pinned on them, the destruction of timber, burning things, destroying property, do you think they did much of that in that period of time?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: I don't think they did near as much of that as what was blamed on to them. I think a lot of that was probably done by, oh, I think it was done by, I don't say who had done it, but I think it was done to reflect on to the Wobblies because they didn't, they wanted to get rid of the Wobblies. And I don't think that all those stories about fires and destruction, I don't think they were done by Wobblies because I was in several camps when the Wobblies were on strike. I couldn't, I didn't see any damage that they'd done, they didn't, they went out on strike and they patrolled the roads going into these camps, what I mean, like if there was a railroad they would put, like they'd say now pickets, you see, and try to stop anybody that was going in there to go to work. They did that all right, but I don't think as far as violence is concerned, I don't think the Wobblies, there might have been incidents, but I don't think it was near what they were given credit for.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: And I never, I never seen any, the only violence I ever saw was that time when they all piled their blankets out in the yard and burned them. I'll never forget that, I was just a kid up here and that made an awful impression on my mind because they had, oh there was quite a crew of men there and they all had agreed that this morning they would go on strike, nobody went to work, see. And they were striking for better conditions in the camps, like clean bunks and all like that. So every man in that camp took his blankets out and he piled them up out there in a pile and they poured kerosene on it and they set them, that pile of blankets on fire. And they burnt every blanket in that camp. I'll never forget that, there was a pile of blankets there as big as this house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it, was it a lot of them, their own blankets they were burning or was it all the company blankets?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: No, it was their own blankets, because the company, that's what they were striking for, the company to furnish the blankets and to furnish steel beds and bunks and mattresses so that they would have a clean, decent place to sleep. Well they got them, they got them. They finally got steel bunks. They were double bunks, but what I mean they were like one bunk above the other. Well they got those, all steel bunks, and they got, they furnished mattresses and pillows and blankets. We had sheet blankets, you know what sheet blankets is, is a light cotton blanket. They furnished sheet blankets and they also furnished wool blankets for coverage. And they were laundered, those sheet blankets were laundered every week. Everybody would gather up each, take your sheet blanket off your bed and lay it there and what they called the bull cook, he'd come along in the morning and pick up all these, this fellow that had done all this work around the camp, called him the bull cook. Well he would come along and collect all these sheet blankets and put them in a big basket and then they'd take them to town and launder them and in the meantime they had another supply there in camp. He'd come along and lay a clean blanket on your bed. See, and you could make your own bed of course, pillow slip the same way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How often change? How often did he change?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Every once a week, every once a week they'd change your sheet blanket, not all the blankets I mean, but your sheet blanket that you slept in. And they were laundered. Well that there was, and then they built bath houses. A big long, they built them, put a big long building and they had some kind of a boiler in there to heat this water and sinks all along, you know. And then they had shower baths in that same building. On one side there'd be a row of showers and on the other side there'd be a row of sinks to wash your clothes in, see, and clothes lines. So then there was heat in there so that you could dry your clothes, see. And they didn't, that wasn't, you know, nobody slept in there so they could open the doors and get the, so that is how they finally got, they got sanitary conditions you might say. They got, then you could wash your clothes. Then the lights disappeared, see. There was no blankets, nothing to carry those. And you could wash your clothes every day if you wanted to and lots of hot water and clothes lines to hang them there and soap there and take a bath every night if you wanted or three times a day if you wanted to. Well, that was different, see. That, there was a, that was a really revolutionary improvement, I think, because, then you had single bunk, see. You slept down here and some other guy slept above. Well, that was all right. Of course, if you'd been here quite a while you'd probably get the lower bunk. New man come in, he'd have to get up above. But still and all, you had a clean sanitary bed. And then they got electric lights. They finally got electric lights. They had their own electric generating plant and those electric lights in all the buildings, see. Even out in the yard. There at Clarkia there, at 42, I think, we had, it was like a small city. They had sidewalks built up, you know, and railings along the sidewalk. Most of them were up off the ground there. And there'd be a railing along the side and there was lights everywhere. You could see where you're going. See, that was the regular city there. You could see lights all around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that these, that the changes did a lot for the, the attitude of the men about the working that they do? Do you think it changed their feeling about the work?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Oh, I don't see how it could help it. I don't see how it could help it because, you know, working in conditions like that, it's bound to give you a, you know, you would have a kind of a, I think you'd have a bad feeling about staying in camp, you know. You just, it was a matter of how long could you endure that, those kind of conditions. That's what it was. It was just a matter of how long could you endure conditions like that. Sleep in a lousy bed. No place to wash. No place to wash your clothes.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: How long could, you take the average man, how long could he put up with that nowadays? How long would he put up with it?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, you can just imagine yourself. Now, you go out there and you're working for so much, them days, it was so much a day or so much an hour. Well, you go out there and you, you just do your best to stay as long as you can and get what they used to call a little stake and then go to town. But it was just a matter of how long you could stand that, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the change in the conditions in the camp, did at the same time, did they change the food at all? Did they make more variety?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Oh, yeah. Oh, heaven yeah. Yeah, the food got, oh yeah, the food, well, it was, it was just a revolutionary change, I'd say, because in later years, like I say, I was called a gyppo cook there for a long time, you know, I contacted the kitchens and we had everything. I'd say we had, well, practically everything as far as meats and fresh vegetables and fresh fruits and,

speaker: Albert Justice
words: the only thing I can think of we didn't have would be crab legs or something like that, but we used to have oysters. Many and many a time I've fried five or six hundred oysters, a great big, you know, for the evening meal, see, that would maybe be like on, maybe Friday night, see, like something like that. Well, we'd have a meat on Friday night, but we'd also nearly always have some kind of a fish dish too. And quite often it would be fried oysters, not every Friday, but quite often it would be fried oysters and some kind of meat. And then other times we'd have like fried halibut or something like that to go with the, you know, and the shrimp salad and things like that. We even got canned shrimp, but we never got crab legs or, but we had chicken, we had turkey, we had fish, beef, pork, and lots of it. And all kinds of fresh fruit, we even had oranges, apples, bananas, grapefruit, we had fresh grapefruit and canned grapefruit, but we found out that the fresh grapefruit, it didn't pay because they wouldn't take time to eat. You know, it's a little, you know, a fresh grapefruit takes a little time to eat it. And they're in a hurry, you know, they come in there and they want to get that breakfast down them and get out of there. Well, so many of them, they wouldn't, we'd cut the grapefruit in half and all like that and put it on a table. Well, there'd be a few of them would eat it, see, but the majority of them, they didn't take the time. So then we got canned grapefruit and put that on in dishes. And they could have, you know, like get out of canned segments. So that went pretty good. And besides, we'd have oranges and bananas and apples and everything that's almost on the market. I just want to get a rough idea of the timing of when the food got a lot more variety.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About when in years did that happen? Was that a long time after the conditions improved in the camps as far as, you know, getting this hot water and all that? Or was it about the same time?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, it was kind of a gradual change, what I mean. It didn't all come at once, you know, like, it didn't all start at once, like from the early days when we just had dried fruits and all like that. It didn't just happen in a day or two or a week or a month or one year. It kind of come gradually. The war had a lot to do with that, you know, because men got scarce and just the natural conditions, I think, had a lot to do with it because it's like it is now. It just was a gradual trend towards getting better food and better housing, better, even better working conditions in the woods, more safety precautions and all like that. It didn't all just happen in just a couple of weeks or maybe a year or two or anything like that, but it gradually, I can remember when we gradually used to start getting like eggs. We'd get one case a week. Well, or maybe if we had a real big crew, we'd get two cases. Well, maybe we could give those men eggs one morning a week, see, and all of a sudden it would have to be hard boiled eggs because that was all we'd have enough for. And then gradually we got more eggs. Well, then after a while we gradually got all the eggs we needed as far as baking and all like that is concerned and putting on the table. And fresh fruits and things like that, they came gradually as things got better and they just kept furnishing like, well, for variety. We used to buy these,

speaker: Albert Justice
words: well, a store bought cookies. They came in big cans like that, that high, see, big round can. I had some of them here, but they're a great big round can and they're that high and there was about, I don't know, about six or eight different varieties of cookies in that can, see. And they were all in there in layers. There'd be a layer here and then there'd be a layer there. Well, they finally got totally furnished in. Well, that helped a lot on our baking because we used to have to bake all the cookies, but we still did bake a lot of cookies anyway. But we used to usually carry about five different varieties of cookies, all homemade. And of course we'd kind of carry them on the table, but we always had cookies and everything like that on the table.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: But,

speaker: Albert Justice
words: now like the Bunks, the steel Bunks, they got those pretty quick right away because the Wobblies wouldn't go to work unless they had those.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember which year that took place and was it 17 when the things got better?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Yeah, that one, the big strike was in 17. And the conditions got better the same year. Oh yeah, they gradually improved, you know. Of course all the camps didn't, a lot of them they hung tough, you know. They didn't want to, of course that was quite an expense for the company to go to work and buy all those steel beds and mattresses and everything like that, springs and all. They didn't all get them right away, but they finally had to come to it. Some of the camps, of course, they put them in right away, but the Wobblies, they hung tough for their conditions.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that there was a lot of variation in the cooking in different camps? Were there both good and bad cooks in the camps?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Oh yeah, sure. Yeah, there was, it's like any other workmen, some of them were good and some were not so good. It's like I say, there was a lot of good, very good cooks up there, a lot of them, excellent cooks. And I knew a lot of them, like Herman and Billy Mush and quite a few of those fellows were real crackers at cooks. And everything they made was good. But you know, them days there was so many camps. There was hundreds of camps up in Idaho there and all that. Well, they all had to have a cook, you know, see. Well, of course they'd have to send in to Spokane here to get cooks out of employment. You know, Joe Maloney, he was there for a long time. Well, they'd hire a cook out of Spokane here and maybe he'd be pretty good, maybe he wouldn't be so good, you know. But you know, cooks as a class are awful drinkers. Most of them, that is those migratory fellows, you know, like, well they come in the camp and a couple or three months they want to go to town again and get drunk. Or maybe they'd bring liquor with them. I've had that, that was another problem that I had in the camp, you know, was that damn liquor. And I guess I'd better be careful. Oh, no, that's okay. I think it's, we all know pretty well that there's something in the game. Yeah, like you say, there was some cooks that were not, well they just didn't care too much for one thing. They didn't figure they'd be there long and they thought, well, get by a couple of months, you know. After they got kind of tired, they wanted to go to town, they didn't care what they put on the table. So yeah, there was quite a variance there in the cooks because there was so many of them, you know, that didn't have to be for all those camps. And most of them were migratory fellows. They'd be down in California and they'd be over in Oregon and they'd be in Washington and Idaho and they just went from camp to camp, you know. And some of them were pretty good, but some of them were not so good. They'd bring liquor out to the camp with them, you know.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, you can't, you can't work and drink, I don't think.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: But like I say, cooks as a rule, the ones that I knew, that is those migratory cooks, they were all of them pretty heavy drinkers. They're like Red Watson. Well, he was an excellent cook. He's one of the best. But not for very long because about a month and that was it for Red Watson. And he had to get drunk and go someplace else and work. Well, but there was a few of us guys that lived there like Ralph Hanson and Billy Mush and we lived there and we had our family. We had to stay. We couldn't very well migrate anyplace because it was a matter of stay there.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: And, but, yeah, there was a lot of difference in cooks. But there was, most of them were good cooks, those fellows. There was another one there by the name of Herman Byers and my wife worked for him and her sister. They both worked for Herman Byers. He was a good cook too. But he was one of those fellows that he didn't stay too long. He was a good cook, a very excellent cook.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the kitchen helped the same way?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Oh yeah, pretty much. That was a problem. That was one of our problems is to get qualified men, that is, kitchen help that would be study and stay with you. You know, they'd like you to hire, like say a second cook, you know. Well, those fellows, they traveled here and there and good study help was a problem, hard to get until then we finally got the girls in the dining room. Well, they stayed better, of course, than the men. And the women were, they kind of solved our problem of dining room help, that is. But that was a kind of a problem for quite a while, to get good because you never knew when some guy was going to say, well, I'm going to town this morning and that would be it.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, somebody had to pick up his hand there and that was usually me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that why they went to women, to women flunkies?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Quite a bit. I think that was the primary reason behind, because the men flunkies were so undependable and then they would drink a lot. They'd go to town, maybe like on Friday night or Saturday night and, well, maybe they'd get drunk and town wouldn't even come back. They'd come back and camp and they'd be drunk. Well, you'd go to get them the next morning and they'd be so darn stupid drunk over there in their bunk they couldn't even get up. They had a lot of that, too, you know. Well, then they'd bring liquor back to camp with them, you know. Maybe they'd bring, well, then they'd be drinking until that was gone. Well, that's bad. You can't drink and you can't keep your mind on what you're doing when you're... But that was one of the problems with men flunkies, they called them, because once in a while you'd get a good man and he would stay.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, I couldn't help it. I couldn't manufacture a man right there. So, things like that. That was one of our problems, the help. There was a few. You'd get, like, the key personnel, like they'd call it. Most of them stayed pretty good. I'd usually try to get local fellows there to work in the kitchen with me and they stayed better than these migratory fellows. But they kind of give me a name up there of being a slave driver. I've heard that quite a number of times, the remarks about that, that shortly justice is a slave driver.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, maybe I was. I don't know. I didn't realize it at the time. But I like to have people do their work and do it right. What I mean, not sloppy, you know. Because they had plenty of time, as a rule, to do their work in a workmanlike manner. But, like I say, maybe they'd have a bottle of liquor over there in the bunkhouse and they'd be nipping on that, you know. And things like that, they'd neglect their own little things, like on the table. They'd neglect to clean the tables properly and things like that. Now, just for one example, you know, we used to keep ketchup on the table. Now, this is just one example. They used to have ketchup on the table in a bottle, you see. And, what's just the sauce and all that salt and pepper along every so often. But, you know, when you use the ketchup, there'd always be a little get around the top of the bottle, see. You've seen that. Well, them darn guys, you know, they wouldn't wipe them off. And maybe they'd leave them go there for a week at a time and they'd get so gosh darn gooed up, you know, under that cap there that it was just terrible. Well, some things like that. I had to watch all them little things, you see. Well, they'd just neglect them because they thought, ah, well, that's, you know, heck with them. Little things like that. And then the salt and pepper shakers, you know. I've seen them darn salt and pepper shakers, the fellas pick them up, you know, with maybe their hands. Some salt and pepper shakers, you ask my wife, they'd get so sticky you couldn't hardly let go of them darn things. Instead of, like in the morning when they're, it's like they were going along the table wiping off the oil cloth, you know, they had oil cloth on the table. Instead of taking a clean damp rag and wipe those salt shakers off, you know, and the ketchup bottles and all things like that, they'd just let them go, see. Well, things like that is, you know, makes you, you go along the table and here are the ketchup bottles all smeared up. Salt and pepper shakers are all sticky. And the other sugar bowls, you see, they'd have sugar bowls with a lid on them. In them days we had a, you know, just a regular sugar bowl with a lid on them. Well, a lot of guys, you know, they'd have their spoon in the coffee and they'd dip in the sugar bowl. Well, maybe there'd be a, they'd leave a spot in there in that sugar bowl. Instead of those guys cleaning those sugar bowls and getting those things, you know, they'd just put the lid on and let them go, see. All those little things annoy you, you see. Well, I was responsible for all that, you see. The guys that kick about that, they kick about it, but what's the matter with that cook in there, see. Well, it's kind of hard to get around to see all those little details when you've got all your other work to do. You'd tell them and they would let you let it go. Well, if you tell them, then they get mad. They get kind of, you know, they don't like to have you tell them that. Pretty soon they get up and quit. And you got so afraid to say anything to them because they'd walk out. Maybe two or three of them would go at the same time, see. Well, that left you in a kind of a bind there when, you know, you're...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Shorty?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Oh, I shouldn't let him hear recording me. Keeps the out.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Is that... You know, I wonder, cooks, they got a reputation for being touchy. You know, that's what, that's what, you know, you hear. And I wonder if that's part of the reason why it's really hard to keep the kitchen going, you know, to the satisfaction of everybody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean the cooks?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Yeah, well, the cooks in the kitchen crew, they have a reputation for being touchy. Well, they are, yes. I think you're, I think you're perfectly right about that. They are. They're quite, they're quite touchy, all right.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: And most of them are, I'd say, sensitive about their, the quality of their cooking, you know what I mean?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: They all think that they're pretty good. Naturally, all these cooks, good or bad, they all thought they were one of the best, you know. It doesn't make any difference whether he could boil a pot of beans or not.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: They all thought they were good, you know, see what I mean?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Naturally, and to criticize their cooking in any way, they're pretty touchy about that. And of course, a lot of them could just laugh it off and say, well, doing the best they can or something like that. Like that guy told me that time, he said, he said, he told me, he said, Kid, he said, you're a pretty good cook, he said, but you can't make beans with a dam. Well, I said, Tom, I said, I said, maybe you're right. I said, I probably got a lot to learn about it, all right. But we just joked about it, you know, but they kick about the soup and they kick about the hotcakes and the heavy hotcakes. One thing they did like, these lumberjacks, and what they were darn particular about was they had their hotcakes hot. And we tried to do that as much as humanly possible, you know, to have them as hot as we could. But even that, some of them would gripe about the hotcakes being cold.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would you keep them hot there?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, it was almost an impossible task to keep them hot all the time. We had great big long griddles there and we'd fry them as fast as we could while they were eating. But, you know, in order to, you had to fry enough ahead in order to put on the table so they'd have something to start on when they came in and sit down. Now, maybe we had 30, 40, like they'd call the setups, you know, 30 or 40 different plates of everything, you know. Well, you had to have 30 or 40 plates of hotcakes to go around. Well, it took, you know, you'd have to put four or five hotcakes on each plate. Well, to keep those hot right off of the stove, it was almost impossible to have them. But then we'd fry while they were eating and the girls would bring the plates back as fast as they could and we'd refill them with hot, with hot hotcakes. But once in a while some guy would get one that wasn't smoking hot and that was kind of one of those things that you can't hardly, not like a restaurant. You just, you come in order hotcakes, he fries your hotcakes and takes it right to you. But where you got maybe 40 different plates in order to go all around this, you just can't have every one of them red hot. But even at that they never got very cold because those girls picked those plates up pretty fast, I'll tell you. And they'd bring them back and we'd take them right off the griddle and put them on the plate and away they'd go out there with them again, you see. So they never got too cold. But anyway, some of those guys got pretty particular.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know when you've got so much food in front of you all the time, three times a day, you get kind of, nothing looks good to you anymore, you know what I mean?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: You got so many things in front of you there all the time. At home you don't probably have that, see. Well, they get a little bit, the crew gets, some of them get a little bit cantankerous about, they don't like this, they don't like that. It might be very, maybe it's alright, all good wholesome food and everything, but when you've got so much in front of you all the time, just like a horse, you turn him into the oats bin, you know, and he'll kill himself eating. But a mule won't do that. You can turn a mule into a bin of oats and he'll just eat so much and he'll quit. He'll not make himself sick. I don't mean these men make themselves sick, but you know, there is a philosophy that when there's so much in front of you all the time, just like a boy with so many toys, he didn't know what to do with them. He don't know what to play with, see. Well, these men, they have so much food in front of them all the time that it kind of gets to be where you... Not all of them, I don't say all the men are that way, but there was always a number of them in the crew that, well, they didn't like this, they didn't like that. Of course, there's always something on the table that you don't like perhaps. You can't put food on the table and have everybody like everything that's on there. There'll be things on there that maybe I didn't like, but even at that there was always something there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you give me an idea of the size of that operation, when you had the real big crew there at, what was it, Camp 35?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: We had all those men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the size of the dimensions of the cooking show there?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: As far as the number of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, I mean, you know, how much did you have to bake for those people, let's say? How much to bake?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: How many... Well, just on an average, like the bread, we had to bake bread for those lunches and everything. Well, it would run around 60 loaves of bread a day, I'd say. Maybe some days a little less, some days a little more, but give or take, I think 60 loaves of bread would be pretty close average and about the same number of pies. And it had to have pies for those lunches and of course sometimes it'd depend a little bit on what kind of pie. Like apple pie, everybody eats apple pie. Well, it would probably take a few more apple pies than it would some other kind, but it would run pretty close to 60 pies a day. Well, then, we always had hot rolls of some kind for the evening meal. Either bread rolls like hot rolls or we'd have bacon, potter, biscuits or cornbread or maybe bran muffins or something like that. Well, we always had some kind of hot rolls. Well, that took probably 400 rolls at least, you know, for that many men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We're talking about 350 men, is that right?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, I think the bread men are along there, give and take, maybe 325, 350 men, but that wouldn't strike a pretty good average. But if they're going to have hot rolls, you've got to figure a little better than a roll to the man, you know. Like say if you got, say, 300 men, you'd have to figure better than 400 rolls. Of course, some don't eat any.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Some will eat bread, but some will eat two or three hot rolls, see?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: So, you've got to kind of figure. Of course, if you run out of hot rolls, it's not a serious thing because you've always got plenty of bread. That is, you better have. And then cookies and donuts and all those things, we kept those coming all the time. What I mean, maybe you wouldn't make donuts every day. Maybe you'd make them today and you'd skip a day or so. And cookies, we'd get three, four, five kinds of cookies on the table. It'd be sugar cookies, molasses cookies, oatmeal cookies, and then we'd make what we call a honey cookie and different things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, we didn't make them all every day, but we kind of kept them in stock, you know what I mean?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: So they could put them on the table. Maybe they'd have about two or three kinds of cookies on the table and donuts and pie and cake and make cake about every day. And then we'd, of course, keep it fresh. And then we'd make cupcakes and all, things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the size of the crew that was involved there at the kitchen? How many people did you have in the kitchen?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, up there at Clarkia, we had, I had two bakers. I had one baker nights and one in the daytime. Ralph, he used to work in the daytime with me. He was the head baker. I had two bakers and I had a meat cutter. Well, a second cook or whatever you call him. And then there was myself. And then we'd have two dishwashers. Because they'd all have to be washed by hand, those dishes. They all had to be washed by hand. And we had two dishwashers. And then I think we must have had about, oh, I'd be from nine, ten girls in the dining room, see. Well, we had about fifty tables. And there was about six men to a table. Sometimes there's more tables. The crew would fluctuate up and down, you know, maybe twenty -five to thirty men from maybe from one day to the next. But it run around fifty to fifty -two, fifty -five tables. And six men to a table. Well, each girl, average, would wait on about thirty men, see. That'd be about five tables.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, the dining room was, we had three dining rooms. We had one that way out of the kitchen. And another one this way and another one this way, see. So they went three ways from the kitchen there. And then the storeroom was back here where we kept all our supplies, see. Well, I think in one of those dining rooms there was seventeen or eighteen tables, see. And there was a table on, there was car camps. And the tables were on each side, see. Six men. And then there was an aisle in between. Well, the girl that had these six tables here, you see, she was close -hauled to the kitchen. She could handle more tables than that girl way down there, see. Because she had, as far as from here, that garage door there. Because she had a long haul. By the time she come back here to the kitchen to fill, refill her dishes, she had quite a hike there, see. Well, this girl here, she could wait on three or four tables while that girl down there was waiting on about two, you see. So, we had to break it up that way. So, there'd be about, oh, I forget exactly how many girls we did have there. It'd be eight or ten. And, of course, it was usually out of that many, usually one of them was laid off or sick or something like that. They'd have to have an extra one, you know, to kind of fill in. But, that'd be about the crew. It'd probably be eight or ten in the dining room, you see. And then three, or two dishwashers. And then there'd be the two bakers. And the meat cutter and myself. And, uh, there'd be twenty people, I guess, while I'm in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You could, uh, could you delegate most of your, a lot of that responsibility? These people that were working under you would take care of their own jobs pretty well?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, I used to hope they would. That is, that was the understanding, that they'd take care of their job to the best of their ability. And, of course, it was my responsibility to see that they did take care of their job as well as they could. And, uh, if they weren't doing their job, of course, there was, uh, something had to be done about it. Because, uh, what it's like I say, all these little details that you kind of had to look after and see that they were being taken care of. And, uh, besides, uh, ordering the supplies, and then I had to do a lot of work besides, too, on a stove there and bringing up the meals, you might say. It was my responsibility to see that the meals were up there and on time and prepared to my best of my ability. And, uh, I didn't only have to do my own job, but I kind of had to, just like any other, uh, supervisory personnel, I had to look to see that the other fellow was doing a reasonably good job, too. Of course, you couldn't always see everything, but, uh... So you spent a lot of your time cooking and also a lot of your time overseeing what was going on. You did... Well, yes, you have to, uh, you had to, uh, you had to do that. You had to, that was all included in your job to see that the whole operation was running, uh, as smooth as possible because, uh, well, you got that much help to, uh... The thing is, if you didn't have any work to do yourself, only look after the operation, that would be different, but you had to do the cooking, too. That is a great majority of it. And I was working on that stove there, and, uh, that, uh, I could dodge here and there, you know, and kind of keep an eye on things, but even at that, there's bound to be some things to escape your attention because... Uh, and the ordering was, you know, that's quite a, that's quite a responsibility, too, to keep supplies on hand. That is, uh, enough and yet not too much of any one thing. Of course, dry staples like flour and sugar and things like that is not so much particular, but, uh, some things you don't want to get too big a supply on hand because they deteriorate. And, like I say, we didn't, in the early days, we had no refrigeration to keep, uh, things, uh, so it was, uh, kind of a battle to know just about how much of these things you were going to have to have and, uh, keep that amount, uh, coming or keep it on hand. But, uh, there's always problems and there was some of them that were pretty hard to solve. That is, uh, uh, you know, some of the, uh, you know, you're working with people, you have all different kinds of people to, you have to be able to get along with people and, uh, see that they, uh, you know, that they get along among themselves as much as possible. What I mean, the crew works together. And, uh, you kind of got to keep an ear cocked for, uh, dissension and, uh, dissatisfaction and so forth like among the, among the, uh, among the help. But, uh, I'd yell at me a slave driver because, uh, I guess I did kind of pressure him a little bit because, uh, I like to see things kept clean. And, uh, I'm kind of a crank about that. And, uh, there was, uh, there was one thing, I don't know whether I ought to mention it on this or not, but, uh, one thing that I used to be, uh, a crank about and I still am, but, uh, I'm very sensitive about that. And, uh, like when we had these men, uh, we say, now go to the bathroom, but, uh, them days it was up on the side of the hill, you know. Well, I hate to see a man or anybody go to the toilet, we'll be playing about it, and come back and start right to work without working. That's something that I, I always, I had many and many an argument about that. And I made the enemies and I, I know I did about, uh, a little thing like that because I used to watch that because, uh, that's something that I don't like, but I used to watch that, see. I'd go up to, of course our laboratories were all up on the side of the hill. Well, they'd go up there in the morning, of course, naturally, and everybody, and, uh, there was always a certain amount of those guys that, uh, I used to keep an eye. They'd come back and they'd put on their apron and go right to work, see. Well, I couldn't, I just couldn't help but mention that to a man. I never could. I always got, of course, I would always, uh, I never come out where everybody could hear it or anything like that, but I would always ask him in private to be sure when you go to the toilet, please, when you come back, be sure and wash your hands before you go to work. Nowadays they got the signs in all the bathrooms and restaurants for employees in a lot of places. They say, wash your hands. Yeah, oh yeah. But that is, um, I don't know, it seemed like some people, not a lot of them, but there was enough that the kind of, it, uh, it bugged me and I, I, uh, I don't mean that I'm any more sanitary or clean maybe than anybody else, but something like that was so plain you could see, you know, they'd put on their apron and go right into work. Maybe they're peeled potatoes or something like that. Well, that's there, I don't like that. I don't mean that I'm any better than anybody else. I don't mean to imply that, but, uh, something like that kind of, there's no excuse for it, actually, because there's always plenty of water there and, uh, and there was no excuse for that. And it was just carelessness, see, and, uh, I think that's very, I think that's very careless. And, uh, things like that, I guess, uh, a lot of fellows would come, they say that shortly that he's a, you can't get along with him. He's a slave driver and all like this, you know. Well, I couldn't get along with anybody with a habit like that. I just couldn't. And I, uh, you'd be surprised how, uh, now there in the kitchen we used to have a wash basin. He had some kind of a, some kind of a niche or something on his feet. And, uh, every night he'd come back in the kitchen and he'd soak his feet in this wash basin, see, where we all had to wash our hands in there. See, well, I didn't, I was probably back in my bunk room there and I didn't notice that until I think Ralph told me about it. God, he said, you know, that guy's soaking his feet in our wash basin there. Oh, I said, no, he said he'd do it every night here before he goes to bed. He comes in here, soaks his feet in that wash basin. Holy smoke. I bet that's, he could have got us something, you know, an old bucket or something. Well, I asked him about it. Oh, well, he said, you're too particular, he said. Ah, you know, he went on to tell me, he said, that won't hurt nobody and all this and that. I said, maybe so, but I said, we can't have that. Well, he said, I've always done that. He said, nobody ever said anything to me about it before. Well, I said, I'm very sorry, I said, but we just can't. I said, if you can get an old pail or a bucket or something, I said, it's all right. Well, I said, we have to wash our hands and face in that basin. And I said, I don't think it's quite right that you should soak your feet in there. I said, well, of course, he got mad. So he persisted in doing that and I had to let him go because there was nothing else I could do. And things like that gives you, he'd probably come to town and say, oh, that's that. He's crazy or something like that, you know. Well, things like that. But I've always been a kind of a crank about being as sanitary as we could with the equipment we had. Of course, we had to mix the bread and things with our hands. But we tried to keep our hands clean anyway. And of course, everything had to be mixed by hand. We didn't have any mixers or nothing. But there's a limit anyway. You know, you can,

speaker: Albert Justice
words: not when it comes to cleanliness. There are a couple of things that you'd said to me that, when we were talking, that made a difference between an average or not so good cook and a good one. And I'd like you to tell me a little bit about them. One was being able to use the leftovers good. And the other one was being able to make the food different. So there was a lot of variety.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Oh, you mean to work up your leftovers?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Yeah, and also to use your food differently. Well, actually, now you've struck something. Being able to utilize your leftovers to a great extent. You can't utilize everything. That's impossible.

speaker: Albert Justice
