words: But being able to utilize these leftovers, like you say, to a great extent. That is what separates the men from the boys. That's one thing right there. That there, I think, is a mark of a good cook and a good planner and a man that can utilize these things to an advantage. Like I say, you can't utilize, you can't use every scrap all the time. But there had to be ways to use up things that came back from the table. That is as much as possible. Well, that is where you have to rack your brain. Instead of throwing it in the garbage can to utilize it again and make it something that's appetizing and something you can put on the table again. That is where it comes. Anybody can go into that storeroom there where there's everything you can imagine and haul out enough stuff for a meal and serve that meal. Almost anybody can do that. But then, like I say, where you've got maybe fifty tables there, there's going to be considerable things come back on those dishes off of those fifty tables. There's going to be meat and potatoes and vegetables and everything come back.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Why are they going to do that?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: That is where it takes a little bit of doing. Well, you can make different things out of comeback meats and things. There's a lot of different ways to utilize comeback meats. Of course, potatoes and things, you can use those up in different ways. But that's where the work comes in. A lot of work comes in utilizing these leftovers because you've got to figure out what to do with them. And it's a lot more work to use something the second time than it is the first time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you stick them in stews or something like that?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, sometimes you can use part of them in a stew or maybe ground up different kinds of hash and things like that. Use them in different ways in cooking. You can use some of those things like potatoes. Like if you had baked potatoes, you can always peel them and use them for fried potatoes and things like that. But vegetables, you can always use comeback vegetables. But really the hardest thing to work over was meat, you know, like comeback meat. You've got to figure out how to use that comeback meat because you can't put it in the garbage can.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do they call that, putting it in the garbage can?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: You told me they had a word.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you say they had something they used to describe that when they throw it out? You said that, was it 86 it or something?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Oh yeah, yeah. Well, a lot of guys, you know, they... Well, like the things coming back from the table now, like from supper.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, maybe there's something on there that's, like I say, that's been worked over, see?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: And they're bringing this back. Well, pretty hard to work it over again a lot of times. So, well, here comes all these dishes back. The cook, he's there of course. There's a guy there helping you take care of this comeback stuff.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, he says, what are you going to do with so and so?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, maybe that's been on the table a couple of times. Well, 86. That means garbage can. Well, you've got to keep away from that 86 because that's where the profit is, where it goes, down that garbage can. But you can't, like I say, you can't just keep working it over and over and over all the time. There's got to be a time when some of it's got to go out. But the best of your ability, that's one of the big jobs is to utilize and work over these comeback, this comeback food, whatever it is. And that sometimes can be time consuming work to pick it over and sort out what you want and what you can use and what you can use in this and what you can use in that. Of course, there's stews and hash and what they call casseroles and things like that, you can work them into that. But it always takes some other ingredients to go with that, usually, to be able to work it over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what about the Gyppo cooking?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: You said that you...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The which?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: The cooking in Gyppo, you said to me that they went to that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the reason they went to that?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Because they had had the troubles keeping cooks that they've decided to... That's about the primary reason. And I think another thing was that, like I say, these migratory cooks, they wasted so much. They were trying to get away from that terrible waste because these fellows coming in for just a month or two or three, they didn't care. They didn't care what... Now, like I say, it was too much trouble to try to utilize these leftover things. They just throw it in the garbage can and go in and get new. Well, of course, that's easy to do that. That's the easiest way to get up a meal, of course, is to just go into the storeroom and you got unlimited supplies of things there almost and just haul out whatever you need and put it on the table. And when it comes back, throw it in the garbage can. That's the easy way to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You see?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: You can clean everything up every night. You don't have anything to...

speaker: Albert Justice
words: They go, what am I going to use that for?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: How am I going to...

speaker: Albert Justice
words: What am I going to put this in?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: I'm going to put that in. You don't have to worry about that. Throw it in the garbage can. Well, the cook houses were running way in the hole. And they were trying to figure out some way to offset that so they could get cooks that would take an interest and stay on the job and not waste so much. Well, they thought if you were, like they say, working for yourself, gyppo -ing, you would be more careful, more interested. And then if you got a percentage of what was made, you would be more interested. You'd have more of an incentive to save things and make... that they give them a certain percentage of what the cook house made. Well, they tried that. But even that, it didn't seem to work out too good. I think I was about the only one, really, that worked under that system for any length of time and made any kind of a success. And I think that's because they didn't seem to... they just didn't take that much interest in it. And then the camps began to dwindle down. There wasn't as many. And the camps got bigger and fewer of them. And there was... they just didn't seem like it. And then the men, they seemed to resent that, especially the union. They resented a gyppo cook. They didn't like me there. They didn't like it because I was a gyppo cook. And they said, well, he's trying to make all the money he can and he's taking it out of our bellies. Well, actually, just the opposite was true because we were putting more on the table. And I think I was a gyppo cook and Ralph was there with me for years and I think he'll tell you the same thing. I might have done some things wrong, but from what I can observe, I think that most of the gyppo cooks, I think, put more variety on the table. But the union didn't like that. They didn't like that. And they were the ones that put up the big squawk about... they didn't like a gyppo cook because they said, well, he's going to make all the money he can and he's going to cut down on food and all like this. But that was not true. That was absolutely not true at all because we had everything in the camp there then. We had everything that you could imagine. It's like I said before, they even had little tiny cans of fruit and sardines and things like that that they would put on the table, not like on Friday morning. We would have cases and cases of sardines in the home. We'd put a can of sardines behind each man's plate, see, so that he could take those sardines out for his lunch. Something for just a little variety, change, something, you know, variation of the lunch. Something besides a sandwich. And they had those little cans of fruit, same thing. They put those behind the man's plate, see, and they could take those to the woods and eat them with their lunch to vary the lunch, you know, make it just a little bit more because a cold lunch is bad enough at best, you know. So they furnished those and oranges and bananas and apples and all those kind of things to try to make the lunch in the woods. Of course, those that didn't take a lunch in the woods, they had the same thing. But it, they strive to please these men as much as humanly possible with the food. But any person in the food business will tell you that it's impossible to satisfy everybody, even you go into a restaurant and order a meal. Well, a lot of people kick on that because there isn't a season quite right. Food is something I think people are more finicky about. What they like to eat and how they like it seasoned. You can take eggs for one thing. People like, like you're going to have fried eggs for a big crew of men in the morning. Now you can't fry every egg separate just to suit every person in that. You've got to fry them just, what you'd say, maybe what you call just average, just fried eggs. Not hard, not too soft. Well, a lot of people, they want their eggs turned over. They want them fried hard. The next guy, he wants just soft. And the next guy, he'll break the yolks and just turn mine over easy and all like that. Well, you can't do that for, you know, you've got the, it's going in the same way with boiled eggs. Well, there you've got a case of, so it, it all boils down to do the best you can. And, but it's, it's a known fact though that after they'd been in camp for three, four months, maybe six months. And the same cook. Oh, I guess they get kind of, I don't know, the food begins, it don't taste near as good as it did at first, you see. Like a new cook comes into camp, maybe. Well, a lot of times, gee, well, he's just, he's great. He's, he's fine. Everything is just right, see. It's a little different than, each cook is a little different, no matter whether he's good or bad or different. But he's different than the one that maybe left. Well, it tastes different, you know, and maybe it doesn't taste any better, but it tastes different. And that's a change. What a change is, is what they need, see. Actually, I don't think any cook should stay in a camp more than about three months because, and then bring in somebody else because it gives these fellows a, because I'll tell you, actually, you know, the food in these camps was a big topic of conversation, really, because something they all talk about is growth. And something different, no matter how good that is, it gets kind of tiresome, maybe the same, my, what I put on the table, maybe it's all right. But it tastes, I have a certain way of doing these things, and after a number of months, well, but a new man comes in, what he puts on the table, maybe it's better, maybe it isn't. But anyway, it's different, see. Two men can go to work and take the same piece of meat and cook it any way you want to, and it'll taste different.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: And they're just like, you go to different restaurants and things taste different. And two housewives, two housewives can get a dinner. One gets dinner here, one gets dinner there. And it tastes different.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you try to make your food as different from night to night as you could? I mean, did you try for variety, for a lot of variety?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: And what this other fellow makes might be the same thing. Maybe it's a beef stew or anything. Well, maybe they're both good, see. Maybe this other man got a better stew than I have or anything. But anyway, it tastes different, see. Maybe they've been eating stew I made for four, five, six months. Well, of course, I can make different kinds of stews and all like that. But this man comes in, he makes his difference. Well, it's a change, you see. And that's what, that's a great thing, is to have a change, have something taste different. Maybe I can make a half a dozen different kinds of beef stews. Well, this man can do the same thing, sure. Well, his tastes a little different than mine, see. Maybe it tastes better, maybe it don't. But anyway, it's different. And they know that right away, see. They can tell. You can tell that yourself. And that's what, because like I say, the food in these camps, when the men stayed for months and months and months, it was a big topic of conversation. Everybody talked about the cook, you know, everybody. All the men, he was either good or bad or he was no good son of a gun or something like that. But because, I'll tell you, when you stay out in one of those camps for a number of months, it gets pretty monotonous. There isn't much to talk about, actually. In them days, they didn't have radios and daily newspapers and televisions and all like that. There wasn't much topic for conversation, only just a kick about the camp foreman and the cook, them two guys, the ones that really got the brunt of all the abuse. Because the foreman, he was a no good slave driving son of a gun and that cook, he was a mulligan mixer and all like that. But it didn't really mean anything, actually. But like I say, they had to talk about something. They didn't have magazines, much to read or newspapers, no diversion, you know. And actually, when you come right down to it, your name is Sam. You take a cook, now we'll see. Okay, he comes into this camp. Well, he's working seven days a week. Around the clock, you might say, he's got a thing of these meals around the clock. What I mean, he gets up early in the morning and he works practically all day. Maybe he gets to lay down in the afternoon for an hour or so. But anyway, he's working every day, seven days a week. He's got no diversion, he's got no... You know, that gets pretty monotonous too after a while. Day in and day out, day in and day out, day after day, week after week. Well, if he isn't pretty interested in his work, you know, it gets monotonous. You begin to think, oh, that's good enough. And you begin to... You know, if a man is not pretty dedicated to his job, and if he isn't... What I mean, if the job isn't a real necessity, he gets a little bit... After a while, he begins to get a little careless. I can realize that. And I think anybody can stop and consider that you take long hours every day, seven days a week. And what do you...

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Like when your work is done in the evening, what are you going to do?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: There's no newspaper, very seldom. There's no radio, no TV to look at, and no nothing. Well, maybe you can go and play cards some place for a little while in the evening or something like that. There's no... It's monotonous. I sometimes couldn't hardly blame these fellows for, you know, bringing a bottle of whisky back with them maybe, something for a little diversion, because it gets monotonous, you know. 12, 14 hours a day every day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long would you be in that camp before you would get to go away on a just days off? How long would you cook before you get time off?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, there was no... There was really no set time. You would just go there and stay until... Well, hard to tell. Maybe something come up you'd want to go to town or maybe you get a toothache or maybe you get sick or something. You have to go into the doctor or something like that. Otherwise, sometimes you'd stay for six months and never... Well, then you'd want to maybe go home and get... But I can remember when I... I went home, I think, for a day, Christmas. And that was about the only vacation I think I had there for... And then later on, we would get... Like on the 4th of July,

speaker: Albert Justice
words: it got so they had roads in the camps there later, you know. And a lot of the fellows would drive their cars in. And they'd go out, you know, for... But in the early... When there was no roads into them camps, you had to either walk in or ride a horse or something. But you'd stay there. My gosh. Until you just got tired and wanted to go to town.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: But... It was pretty monotonous because the only thing you could do is take a walk around through the woods, maybe.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, how...

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Oh, yeah. Here, something. Well, I tell you, I got to go change that warm. I'll be back.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Because, you know, it did get pretty monotonous. Well, what I was going to ask you about was how that gyppo worked when you gyppo'd the kitchen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was that set up for you as far as the arrangement with the company for payment of people and that sort of thing?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, the way that it was set up, the company... Of course, they had a bookkeeper, you know. And they kept track of all the books, all the income and all the algo. And each man was charged so much a meal per day. That is, so much for each meal, like breakfast. And it kept going up and up. I forget just what it was, a dollar and something a meal. But anyway, the company bookkeeper, he kept track of all these meals, how many meals were served. Maybe like for breakfast it'd be 300. Well, if there was 300 and four or five, of course, we had to check on those meals, you see, pretty close. That was... Because everybody that eat there was supposed to give credit to the kitchen for that meal.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, all the supplies and all the labor costs had to come out of what was taken in for those meals during the month, see. And then what was left, that was mine. I had to pay all the labor cost in the kitchen and all the supplies, food and everything, whatever that come to, then that was deducted from what was taken in, you know, by the men's board, see. Like if you were paying a dollar and a quarter a meal, not a day, but a meal. Well, the company bookkeeper then, he kept track of all that, see. I didn't have to worry about that. I ordered the supplies and supervised the help and then we'd take an inventory every month, the last of every month, you see. We'd take inventory of all the supplies that were on hand. Well, of course, that was all figured in with the... against what we'd bought, you see. All the canned goods and all the meats and all the... Of course, not the equipment, not any of the equipment, but just the things that had been... I didn't have to pay any rent or anything like that on the kitchen or the equipment. That belonged to the company, of course, but that was all figured... what came in and what was paid out. And whatever difference was that, I got that. Of course, if I didn't... I was guaranteed wages anyhow because the law requires that, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did what you made very much from month to month?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, I used to do pretty good, yeah. I did pretty good there. Of course, you had to have a big crew in order to make good. You couldn't come out on a small crew because, like I say, volume is what you had to have to make money, you know, like on any other business, practically. But if I had... if we had a big crew, I used to make pretty good money. I made as high as $1200 a month there. Of course, it would vary. It wasn't always like that, but... And that is the thing that the union didn't like that. That got... I don't know how it got around, but anyway, it would get around. You can't keep those things, you know. Even the kids in town would throw it up to my daughter in school there. You know, they would... And I don't know how in the world because I never told anybody. I never told... That was one of the agreements that I would not reveal one way or the other, whether I was going broke or not, but anyway, it gets out somehow. And anyway, but... Of course, I don't... I didn't make that much money every month, but I made pretty good there for a while. I did. I did pretty well. And of course, I worked hard. I done two minutes work, you might say. And that was the argument that I put up to the union. I said, they wanted me to work for wages, see, see. And I said, well, I'll do that. If you will let me work eight hours like you're working, see, the same as you work. The union men in the woods, I said, I'll do that. But I said, you have to put another cook in here for another shift because I said, you can't do this work in one eight hour shift, nobody, because the meal, you can't do it. I said, if you will put another cook in here, I will go for straight wages and work one shift and this other man can take the other shift, see. Oh, gee, they wouldn't do that.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, I said, what do you want me to do? Work two days every day for one day's labor?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: I said, I'm working anywhere from 12 to 14 hours a day every day in this kitchen. You can't get away from it. And I said, you wouldn't want to go out there and work 12 hours a day in the woods for the same wages.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: I said, would you?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, that's different, see. This is more like, that was different, that was different. But they said, they don't like the gyppo. But they were gyppoing out there. They were all working by the thousand feet, what they call by the thousand. The sawyers got so much a thousand feet and all them. But they didn't want the cookhouse to be gyppo because they said, well, they're taking it out of our stomachs. But it finally boiled down to the thing that they, they got me out, there's no doubt about that. The union, they put the pressure on the company and during the war there, they could almost dictate the policy because, to the company because lumber was number one priority and they had to have lumber and then they wanted lumber and they threatened to strike that camp. Well, that was the biggest camp in the country and they didn't want a strike in that camp because that would be a very serious thing for the lumber industry because they were getting out a lot of timber there.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Because the union sent a committee down to Lewiston, see, that was the headquarter. They sent this committee, this Johnson up there, they called him, he was the head organizer there in Bogue. Well, they sent a committee of three down to the manager at Lewiston. His name was Billings and they told him, they said, if that cook doesn't join the union, we're going to strike up there at camp. Well, that was a serious thing, you know, they didn't want that to happen. So Billings came up to camp one day, he and a couple of other fellas, so they were there for lunch and after lunch he called me in the office there and, God, he said, you know, we've got a problem here and he said, the union, you know, they were pretty strong, they'll shut this camp down unless you join the union. He told me, he said, there's been a committee down there and they'll talk to me about it and they told me that they want this camp 100 % union, see. And they said the kitchen crew is about the only one that's not lined up, not organized. And he said, if you don't join the union, he said, of course the rest of the crew don't join, you see, they don't join. Well, he said, God, I don't like to see you leave, we can't shut this camp down.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: He said, why don't you join the union?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: I said, no, Mr. Billings, I said, I won't join the union, but I said, I'll tell you what I'll do, I'll give you a week to get another cook. I said, I've been here a long time and I'm tired anyway and I said, I think it'd be a good change and so I think, I told him, I said, I think I'll just go home for a while. I said, it'll be a good thing. I said, they'll be more satisfied maybe and I don't like to have this dissension anyway and I knew there was a lot of talk there. And of course, well, he said, that's up to you. He said, of course, that's your decision. Well, I said, I'll stay a week and that'll give you a chance to get another man. I said, I'll do what I can to... So I did, I told them, I said, you get another man and I'll stay till he comes. So I did.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: How long did it take to get those up?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, in three or four days, he said, oh, God Almighty, he said, I never, we said, I never saw that many men in my life before. He said, I ain't gonna stay. He only stayed a couple of three days. Well, they got another fellow, Lyle Pearson, I knew him real well. They got him out of Spokane. He's a good man too. Lyle was a real good man, but Ralph told me, he said, he was drunk the night he got there. And he said, the next morning, he got up to get breakfast. He said, he come in and got me. He said, I got Ralph. He said, you come on out and give me a hand to get breakfast. He said, I'm not feeling too good. Ralph said he was drunk when he got there that night. But anyway, Lyle stayed on and I guess he got a good job. Ralph told me, he said, Lyle did a real good job, but they cut the crew right down there after, oh, just a short time. I don't think it was more than a couple of weeks or something like that. So they didn't have too much of a crew there after that. But Lyle, he stayed on I guess. And he was a good man. Lyle was a good man. But like I said, he used to drink a little. But he was a good cook. And I guess he done a good job from what Ralph said. He stayed there and he got sobered up. But anyway, they cut the crew way down so that he didn't have that problem.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was the last that you cooked in the woods? Is that the last that you cooked in the woods? Me?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: No. Lumber Company. No, I went back and cooked for the potlites again after that, up at Elk River. I was up there for, well, let's see, I bought the restaurant. No, I didn't either. I can't just remember now how those things went. But I went back for the company at Elk River. They were stationed right in town there in a car camp. And I went up there. I was staying at home there and they called me down to the office and they said they needed a man up at Elk River there. And so I said, well, I'll go up for a while. So I went up there and it was right in the bed of winter. So I stayed there a while and then I, I don't know, I was there four or five months I guess up there. So then I negotiated for the restaurant there in town. This woman that was running the restaurant, she'd been in there for a good many years. She was getting quite old and she wanted to get out. So I thought, well, guys, I'm going to try that. So I bought the restaurant there from this lady and we ran that for, I think, about two years. And we did pretty good there. We did real well, in fact. But it was right during the rationing time, you know, like meat and all those kind of things. It was awful hard to get. All the restaurants were having trouble getting meat and, well, eggs and milk and all those things. They had to have red points, you know, for things like that. And they were awfully hard to come by and meat was something that, and of course the camps had been, they had been cut back, real drastic on their meat supplies of all kinds. And it was getting pretty rough in camp too to put out a meal. I remember up at 32 there, the last order of meat I got while I was there. I think all it was was the front shoulder.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: You see?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, it was a big one. It was a big front shoulder.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, what are you going to do with that?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, about the only thing you can do with it is make a big stew out of it. So I was kind of disgusted anyway and was tired.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you left the camps, you left the camps right in that period of time?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: We lived in Bovill and I went home. I stayed home for a while and they called me to go up to Elk River there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that where the restaurant was in Elk River?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: No, the restaurant was in Bovill. So we bought the restaurant there in Bovill and we stayed in that. But it was awfully hard to get, well, anything. You couldn't hardly buy soap or anything like that. It was terrible. And the meat was, I used to just have to go around and scrounge the meat where I could. Well, even the fellas out in camp were not getting much meat then. They were cut down way back on meat. And of course I knew all them lumberjacks around there, hundreds of them you might say. Well, they'd come in from camp and they'd want a meal. They'd come in the restaurant there.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: I said, hey guys, shorty, you got a T -bone steak or something? I said, where would I get a T -bone steak?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, I said, come on now, give us something. We want to have an old meat out in camp for a week and all like this. Well, I'd scrounge around and get them something. I had ways and means there. Well, you know, on the menu, we were not supposed to have more than one meat item on the menu, see. Well, I'd have macaroni and cheese and stuff like that, you know, but they didn't want that. They wanted a steak or some pork chops or something. No, they'd come in there. I was working myself to death in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you get meat from the farmers?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, this don't go on record too much. I was getting meat from the farmers, all right. That's what I would figure. Yeah, I had to. There was nowhere else because you had to have points to get this meat from a legitimate source and I couldn't get them points. Because they had to have points for eggs, cheese, milk. They're red points for all those kind of things, like all this animal stuff like cheese, milk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this at the end of the war, right after the war?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Yeah, I was right during the 40, 42, 43, long in there, I think it was. Yeah, my memory, I guess, I think I can't exactly remember. But we were in there. But I remember, gosh, I used to go out at night and get farmers down around the ridges, kendries down there. They'd come into town and they'd come in there and say, well, I got a so -and -so if you want it, I'll bring it in for you. Well, bring it in for myself. I'd bring in a couple of hogs and I had a big storage plant in the back there and I used to cut all my own meat there. Well, I had to, then I bought meat down at the dairy there from Anderson there in the store. He was pretty good, he'd give me a lot of meat there, but I really wasn't entitled to it. But I had a lot of meat there, all right, but I didn't have it on the menu. Oh, but a lot of guys were doing the same thing. Oh, they were all doing the same thing. I know those fellas in Moscow there, why they were going to get meat from these farmers around. I thought, well, you couldn't, if you didn't use any more meat than what you'd get for those points, you wouldn't have had it.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: But that was kind of a dog -eat -dog proposition. I got tired of that too, fighting around trying to get a little meat and some eggs and you couldn't even buy soap. I sold cigarettes there. Well, they'd come in with all these oddball brands of cigarettes, you know. In order to get a case of, you know, the good cigarettes like what was selling Lucky Strikes and so forth like that, in order to get a case of those, you had to take four or five cases of oddball like Domino's and all that kind of darn stuff. Well, I had cigarettes like that piled up under the counter there, nobody would buy them. You put a package of Chesterfield or something out there on the counter, well, they'd go like that. But then here I had all these other darn things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'd say, do you want some Domino's or whatever they were?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: No, nobody'd buy them. Well, here they were all, but in order to get a case of saleable cigarettes, I had to take three or four cases of that junk, see. Same way it was soap. To get a case of decent soap to wash dishes with and so forth, I had to buy a lot of other darn junk. I just used to throw it out in the garbage. But that took advantage of it that way, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, so it was after that that you came up Spokane?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Yeah, we sold the restaurant and came to Spokane. But it was such a dogfight to get things and I just got disgusted. I was working with them because we had the train crews there, see. The company didn't seem to have any place to board them at that time and we had the train crews there. And sometimes there'd be 25 or 30 of them. Well, they wanted breakfast around five o 'clock so they could, they wanted to get out early in the morning. I had to be down there and get breakfast with old guys that haven't, that haven't ready at five o 'clock in the morning. They wanted to eat. Well, pretty long hours, you know. Maybe they'd be away late in the evening coming in, you know. They'd work overtime. Maybe they wouldn't get in there for supper at seven, eight o 'clock. Well, I'd stay there and have supper for them. Maybe there'd be 10, 15 of them, 20 of them maybe and go on supper. No place else to eat. Well, I was putting in pretty long hours there. I was making money, all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which, is that that place on Main Street there now near the fire? Is that the place on Main Street in Bovill near the fire department?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Yeah, that's right there. It's still there, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You been in there?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Yeah, well I ran that for two years then. High ceilings. And I don't know who's got it now. Bailey had it for a long time. Yeah, I don't know who's got it. There was a local fellow who bought us out there and I don't think he was in there long, but, and then I guess it changed hands several times, but... And then I had a lot of the company men there. And we had a big business there. I was, and I couldn't get no help. You know, that was right during the war you couldn't hire. I had a couple of girls there, but they, oh, they got married. And my wife and I there, we tried to, but we couldn't do it all. I hired what I could, but it got pretty hard to find any, you know, they could go, they wanted to go to the city. And there just wasn't any young people around there to hire anymore. They seemed like all the young people were going to the city. And I want to ask you about the, you said that on the holidays in the camps there, when you had the kitchen, you really laid out some spread on the big holidays.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On the big holidays?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Yeah, you said on the holidays in the camps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said like on, was it Christmas and Thanksgiving, you had a big, real big spread?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, yes, now like, well, Christmas and maybe New Year's. And we would give them, of course, now this wasn't really way back in early days, but, well, probably it started in around about the 30s, long in there. Well, for Christmas, we wouldn't have, well, just about everything. We'd always have roast turkey and fried oysters. We'd always have that for Christmas dinner. And the Christmas dinner then, of course, the men didn't work on Christmas. We'd have that at noon, usually as the big dinner, probably at noon. We'd always have roast turkey and we'd have fried oysters. As a rule, they liked fried oysters. Well, we'd have all the trimmings, like they say. We'd have turkey and cranberries and sweet potatoes and mashed potatoes and a couple of vegetables and two or three kinds of pie, the mince pie and pumpkin pie and maybe nearly always a soft pie, lemon pie or something like that. They usually had about three kinds of pie. Then we'd have some kind of plum pudding or something like that, you know, a regular Christmas pudding. And, of course, everything else that went with the, you know, potatoes and gravy and vegetables and desserts. And a lot of times they even give them cigars and a package of cigarettes behind their plate and the oranges and apples and all those things. And everything almost that you could imagine for a Christmas dinner. And a lot of times it'd be the same thing on New Year's. Now I know Axel come in up there one time when we was up to Clarkia there and he said, you know, the boys have been doing a real good job of getting the timber out this winter. And he said, I think we ought to give them a little treat.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: He said, why don't we give them a big dinner for New Year's?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, I said, that's fine. We'll do that. So we did. We gave them another Christmas dinner on New Year's. We had to put near the same thing, a little variation, but I think we had fried chicken that time and everything that went with it, you know, potatoes and gravy and dessert and all that stuff. I didn't have cigars and cigarettes, I guess, but we had everything, pies and puddings and cakes and everything on the table. He said the boys had been doing a real good job, the Ritter, he said, and they'd been working hard in the deep snow and all like that.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: He said, why, what do you think?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: He said we'd give them another big dinner. Well, I said, fine, we'll give them everything we got. I said, well, so we did. We gave them another New Year's dinner. And so they were happy with that and that's the way, but of course in early, early times they didn't get all those things.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: But I think that was the last Christmas I was up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have much to do with the foreman, like Axel? Did you work much with him?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Oh, you had to work, yeah, you bet, right, with the foreman, yeah, you betcha. Like your time, you know, because like I said before, you know, they wanted these meals on time. And that is within, give or take, maybe a minute, but not more. And I'd check with Axel every morning. Of course, he'd always come through the kitchen. Their office was in one end of the, they'd always have to come through the kitchen into the, where they always eat right on this end, the dining room there, see. And they'd come through from the office and I'd check with him sometimes, a couple of times a day or whenever I'd happen to think of it, with our time, see. Of course, I'd try to keep my time with his. He was the camp boss and if I was a minute off, well, we'd check on him. Oh yeah, you had to work close with the foreman because he was the, he was the, he was the big cheeser out here. And actually, I'd check with him every morning and lots of times every evening when he'd come in. And we'd try to keep coordinated that way because them guys didn't want, they wanted breakfast, they wanted at that time. They didn't want it 25 minutes to seven or, same way at night. They put it particular about that night meal, boy, they wanted that right on time. I think it's pretty interesting that in this real big camp they had the two of you there, both of you, because from what I've heard, Axel Anderson has quite a reputation as a good boss and you have quite a reputation as a good cook. Well, I don't know about that. I had a lot of enemies too there, there's no doubt about that because, like I say, the Union, but maybe they have another version of why I got out of there. But as far as I know, I didn't get what I mean. I wasn't let out or anything, I wasn't, but I would have been, I would have been let out because rather than shut that camp down, they would have let me go, I know, and I couldn't have blamed them for it because, but I wasn't going to be that stubborn because there was no sense in it. And anyway, I thought that, I'd been there, you know, a long time and I was tired and, like I said before, I think a change is good for the crew and it's something, it's different. It's just like you getting up in the morning, maybe you have ham and eggs or something. Ham and eggs is very good. Well, you probably don't want it 365 days in a year. You'd rather have maybe a bowl of mush sometimes. Well, it's that way with a different cook coming in, you know. Maybe he's better, maybe he ain't better, but anyway it's different and I thought, I thought it was time anyway for a change that way. Well, the same way when I went up to Elk River, I relieved that fellow up there. Well, gee whiz, I relieved Shorty Trimble one time, you know. Well, Shorty was a real good cook, there's no doubt about it, one of the very best and he was a lot better than I was at that time, I know that, but anyway, I went up there and relieved Shorty and, well, everything was fine, see.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Because I'll tell you, Shorty was one of these physical culture guys, you know what I mean?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: He was quite a guy to take his physical exercises and he had, he got so, I guess, somewhat higher, now I'm not saying this will go on record or anything, but he got so he was paying more attention to his exercises. But anyway, out in the storeroom, you had a place fixed up with a punching bag out there and some of these, and he'd be out there, you know, working on them and they got so, I guess, that they, I don't know, I guess he kind of got neglected a little bit, but anyway, I understand they let him go and so I went up there and took over for Shorty Trimble, but he was, Shorty was one of the very best cooks in the country, I'd say that, but he just was getting tired, I guess, anyway, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When do you think you got to be a, how many years do you think it took you to really master the kitchen so you became a top -notch cook?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: I mean, I mean about as far as like when you started, you must have had a lot of learning. I tell you, you learn something every day, Sam. Cooking is something that, it's just like going to school, you can learn something every day and you can work in the kitchen for your lifetime and you can still pick up things that... I've had fellas come along practically on the bum, looking for a job, put them to work washing dishes, you know, and those guys, some of them, I've even had them show me pointers, you know, that I never, they're pretty good things too, that just guys like that, you know, see, they pick that up someplace else. There's always somebody that knows, you know, that knows some things too about, no matter, he might be a, he might be a tramp cook, but he might have some pretty good ideas too, you know, that you've never thought about.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: And I've had guys come along on the bum and I work in the kitchen, I haven't had them with me for a while, washing dishes or doing something, and they'd see you doing something, they'd say, well, Shorty, why don't you ever try so -and -so, do this or that? Well, why don't you try so -and -so, by golly, now that's a good idea, why didn't I think of that?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: But, you know, like I said, you can't never master at all, I don't think there's a chef in the world, I've worked with some good ones too, at the hotel down here, I've seen six chefs come and go down at the hotel while I was there, and they work pretty good ones, most of them. If they hadn't been there, they wouldn't have been there.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: But even at that, they didn't know at all, you know, see?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: We had some, like I say, we had some good top -notch chefs down there, and I was in the purchasing department, I didn't have much to do with the chefs, only to order this.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: But I used to be up there all the time, I'd watch some of those fellows, and even I knew some things that they didn't know, see?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: I don't claim to be a chef or anything like that, I'm just a plain cook, but I used to watch some guys sometimes, and I'd see them doing things, and I thought, well, now, I'd be gosh darned if I don't think I got a better way than that, but I never said anything because I had nothing to do with that department.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: But even at that, you know, even as good as they were, they didn't know at all, see?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: And I don't think anybody can learn all about food, no matter if you live to be a hundred years old, because, but like you say, to get to be a top -notch, you can get to be pretty good, but there's always things that you, like I say, I've seen some darn good cooks in those camps, excellent cooks. And, but even at that, they didn't know everything, and I've seen guys come along that had little ideas and pointers, how to do things a little faster, and by gosh, you know, I don't know, but even at that, you can learn something every day, that is if you're, if you're in the mind to. Of course, some guys just go along, they don't pay no attention, but if you're interested and want to improve your skill, you can learn, you can pick up cookbooks and read them and pick up pointers and lots of, there's many ways to learn things about cooking, because food is so, so many varieties and so many ways of preparing things, eggs and all kinds of meats, and you could study for two or three lifetimes, I don't think you'd ever know at all, because... How do you think cooking for a lot of men, or a lot of people, compares with cooking for just like a chef does, he just makes a dish for one person, as far as, can you cook, can you cook almost as good for a lot of people as you can, for just making it individual -like, or is it just... Well, I don't know, I think in a lot of ways you can, if you have the proper facilities and the ingredients, and you have the time, I think you can... I know I used to, I could go ahead and get a better meal there than I can here at home, but of course there's a lot of things that they say should be put together in small portions, I imagine maybe you can do better cooking for a small group than a large... I've cooked for the army, and like I was talking about Herman Gutschalk there, he told, he said, he wouldn't cook for a big crew, he said, you can't do the same, he said, you can't, you just... And he said, I'm not gonna take on more than I can do and do it right, and he was one, he thought that you could, and maybe you can, perhaps you can, I wouldn't want to say exactly, because there's some things, certain kinds of cakes and things, if you're gonna... You can do better by making those, you know, really in a smaller batch I think, but I've seen the time when I made 50 angel food cakes, you know, well, you couldn't mix them all at once, you had to mix those, I'd make about maybe 10 at a time, see, and angel food cakes is pretty particular to make, well it didn't make them too often, but... That is one thing that, I don't know, maybe these big bakeries have a way of making them now in big, but of course we had to do everything by hand, you know, we didn't have any, and I think the things like that perhaps, like a sponge cake, that's pretty, that's something that takes a little care, and I think you can do better by making not too much, not too much at a one time, but...

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Of course, roasting meats and things like that, you can roast a large amount of, you know, we used to roast, like turkeys of course, we'd roast maybe 15, 18 turkeys, but of course they were in the... But things you have to mix and put together, I guess perhaps, certain things, certain things that is better, alright, and smaller amounts.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much oven space, how many ovens did you have there at the camp? How many ovens did you have there at camp?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, let's see, we had three, six, I think we had seven. We had two kitchens, you see, we had one in the big kitchen, we had two, three oven lang, they were big lang stoves that were made in Seattle, three oven langs, we had two of them, and then there. And then in the other kitchen we had one big stove with one big oven in there, that was a great big, just a big single stove with one big oven, so that gave us seven ovens there, all together.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: And we, that was, well, you might say the main kitchen, and then the smaller kitchen down there, but more of an auxiliary kitchen or something like that, but... Yeah, we had, I'm pretty sure, seven ovens there, and I think they were, I'm pretty sure there were three oven langs, and...

speaker: Albert Justice
words: But, it was, you know, you got your meats to roast, you got your pies to bake, and you got your bread to bake, and your rolls to bake, and you've got cookies and all those things, takes quite a bit of oven space. You got plan, you got the kind of plan, your oven, what you can do this day in your ovens, Ralph and I, we talked that over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The night before?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, sometimes the night before, maybe in the morning. Ralph, he'd usually take inventory in the dining room, what pastry they had, you know, see. He'd go in after breakfast and size up what he thought we needed.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: There was no use mixing up a batch of something if you couldn't bake it. But there was always bread and pies that had to go in, and meats and things like that, and then sometimes there'd be big puddings and things like that that go in, you know, baked puddings, you know, like... Well, you'd have the kind of figure on, you know, just what you could do and what you couldn't do. Well, maybe the next day we could pour it on and bake a lot of cookies, see. But I just like planning anything else. You had to plan your moves, what you could do, and... Yeah. And... You had to work together with your baker and the like of that, because he had a lot of things to go in those ovens, you know.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, help of any kind, what I mean. No mixers or anything like that. Everything had to be done by... Everything had to be done by hand. Bread mixed by hand, and... But that's the way it was done, and that's the way everybody... They didn't think anything about it. But now I go down here and I see these, and I like it in the hotel down there. They had, in the bake shop, they had every mechanical convenience you could think of, you know. A bake shop there. And it makes it easy, and faster too. You can... Great big bread mixer there, big around, you know, you can mix the bread there. Rolls, automatic machine to make the loaves of bread and make the buns, and they just throw the dough in there and they come out. If you're gonna make hot rolls, they come out, everyone exactly alike, and the bread exactly the same, and all, everything is... The baker, he just, about all he had to do was mix it up. That is, put the ingredients in and turn on the mixer. Well, that's quite a lot different than having to mix that bread by hand, you know. Oh, it's, you know, of course, never seen that. You can't hardly visualize how that was, the way it was... But we had problems too, I'll tell you that. Some serious problems in those days, and I'll tell you what one of them was, and that was flies. That was a bad problem in those camps for a long time. More so, years ago, when they had the horses, you know. Because naturally, and then another thing, there was no sanitary, no sanitary facilities of any kind, like outside, there was no... You take like, a big crew like that, you know, takes a good many outside facilities, you know, to take care of. Well, flies were a problem, I'll tell you. You can't visualize, you can't imagine, if I'd tell you, you couldn't hardly believe the flies that would accumulate around the camp like that, you know. The horses, for one thing, brought them. And of course, the smell of the food, and there was no, I don't know, there just didn't seem to be any way to get rid of them darn things. We used to rack our brain with all kinds, they used to make fly traps, you know. There was a thing that used to make fly traps. We'd use them and set them around the different spots around the... Of course, the kitchen is where the darn flies really were the most... didn't bother anything out in the barn or anything like that, but around the kitchen is where they were the, you know, the flies on the food. And they were a problem, they were a big problem, and we never was able to, of course, we had no means to combat the darn things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did fly trap work? What was that?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: A fly trap, well, I'll tell you. They used to make... they used to make them in camp. They'd be just a big cone, see, just a great big cone, maybe as big around as... all bigger than that stool, see, and one big cone. Then there was a place underneath where the flies could go in, it would set on a base like this, you see. That cone would set on a wooden base like that, it was held up off of there a little bit. And then inside of that cone there was another little cone, see, and there was a hole in that little... that inside cone, a little hole up in the top out there, about big as the end of my finger, just big enough for a fly to get through, see. Well, those two cones come down here, see. Well, they were... we could put something there on that for kind of a bait, a piece of meat or anything like that, see. And those flies would congregate, see, on that platform there. Well, naturally they fly up, see. When they wanted to get out, they'd naturally fly up. They'd never come back out the way they went in. They'd go up through this little hole, see, in that... well, then they were in there, they couldn't get out. There was quite a space between the little cone and the big cone. Well, that's where they would be trapped in there, see, in between those two cones, because they never knew enough to go back down through that little hole, see, where they went in. They'd go up through that... they'd come down here, maybe they'd eat some of that meat or something, and of course, naturally when a fly goes, he'd go up, see. Well, 99 out of 100 would go up through that little hole in that inside cone, and then he'd be up in there, and they'd never get out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How well did that really keep the flies down?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, no, it didn't keep them down. It caught a lot of them. It could get... that thing would be... you'd look at that thing in a couple of days and it'd just be packed in there with flies, see. Thousands of them, but that didn't hardly make a dent in them. And then they'd empty that, see, and you could... they were fixed so you could empty it and throw them out in the... and then put it back, and we had several of them sitting around, but even at that, they... flies come... millions, you can't imagine. And then they'd get in the kitchen, of course, you couldn't keep them out. They would... where there's so many, they would use... and then they'd get in the dining room and... and they'd get on the food, you know, like... oh, like before supper, you had to put things on the table, you know, like their... all the cold things like the cookies and cakes and things like that or anything that happened to be on the... anything that happened to be put on the table before you put the hot food on. You had to have those things... well, maybe you had a nice fresh cake, see. Well, you have icing or something on that cake, you know, see. Well, you had to put that on before the supper's ready, you know, you set it on the table. Well, damn flies, they light on that and of course they put flyspecs on that, see. You know, there's a terrible... we used to try to keep them but you couldn't keep them away and despite everything we'd do, there'd be some of them, get on some of them, something. Maybe not on all the tables but maybe... maybe two or three flyspecs on the cake. Well, maybe just be a couple of minutes before the men come in that they'd, you know, the damn flies are flying all around. Well, we'd dinner, we had sprayers, you know, one of those things that we'd go around and spray. Well, you can't get that on the food, you know, you had to be careful. Well, we'd go around the windows and spray them where we could and of course some of the darn things, you had to be careful they didn't drop down on the end of all of the food, too, you see. You'd spray them and they'd circle and fly around and maybe they'd light down into a dish of... maybe you got some fruit sitting there, oh, they'll lighten that fruit. Well, you got a dead fly. Well, that thing, you had to be awful careful.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the heat? Was that a problem in the kitchen with all the stoves going? Was it real hot in there?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, it was a big thing. In the summertime, I'll tell you, terrific. Terrific. Yeah, you bet. Of course, we'd have the doors open, what we could, screens on them, have the doors open. And we always had one of those things on top. Oh, it was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A big type fan?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, yeah, it was a thing. It circulated all the time and it'd go round and round. It wasn't a fan, it didn't have any power or anything, it was just a natural draft going up through there that kept that thing circulating all the time. It was probably as big around as... Oh, big around that stool there and it set up about so high off of the roof there and it circulated all the time, 24 hours a day. But anyway, it gets terrible. Just imagine standing over them big ranges there frying pork chops. And I'd... We used to... Summertime, we'd have a towel or something, we'd wrap it around our neck, you know, and sweat it just running right down off your nose there. And you'd wipe yourself with that towel, you know, and you'd stand there frying pork chop or steak or whatever had happened to me. But there was no way to get away from that heat, you just had to take it, that's all there was to it.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: And it was just a matter of... Like I say, it was monotonous, seven days a week and you had to be pretty dedicated in order to stay and, you know, a fella gets... You just get tired and no diversion, no... After supper you'd go out and take a walk down the road, around the walking road. But you were probably pretty tired, you didn't want to do that even.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: There's one more thing that I thought of and that's... What the kitchens were like when you first started in... What kind of facilities you had, they had back around 1920. And in there, were the kitchens real small then and a whole different... Well, I'll tell you, Sam, most of them those days were built out of logs, they were just a big long log building. That is the way back in... They were just log buildings, see.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: And... It would be like the kitchen and be maybe like in this end, you know, and the dining room and that end, see. Well, sometimes there'd be a petition, a little petition between the kitchen and dining room but very often there was no petition. Just the kitchen here and a stove against... Maybe this was the back of the... The back wall. Well, your kitchen and your stove would probably be against that wall and you'd have a... Maybe your bake table where you made your bread and things would be on that side and... And then maybe out beyond you'd have a storeroom or something like that for to keep your supplies but... Most of them were just the kitchen here on this end, the dining room on that end and then be a bunk maybe in one corner for the cook and the flunky or something like that, maybe a couple of... But most of them were just long, big long log buildings and... And... That's where you... Everything was prepared right in that... And then of course those log buildings, they... After a couple of years, they'd get infested with mice, you know, and that was another problem in those days with mice and rats. There used to have a lot of mice and quite a few rats because the rats would naturally get into those logs and the mice would get in the logs, you know, after they were there several years. And then that was a problem because everything had to be mouse -proof and covered and anything, you know, mice would gnaw their way into... You had to be very sure and very careful that everything was... You put away was in such a shape that the mice couldn't get into it. Because they were... Some places they were pretty bad because... And we'd have mouse traps sitting around. Well, here comes more coffee. Well... I think we should wrap it up. We've...

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: We're not wrapped up?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: We are. We really have a... Have one of these and... Remember when people... Everybody had mouse traps. Mouse traps. And we had mouse traps in the camps in the early days and we'd set them around here and there and... We'd be maybe sitting around there in the evening and click... You'd hear a mouse trap. Well, then a lot of times they maybe had to be a mouse trap down there someplace to get overlooked and the mouse would get in there and maybe be in there for quite a while. And then all them things that they had to do. And then we had other things there. We had them darn pack rats, you know. They'd get in. And they were a terrible thing. Pack rats, they used to call them. Pretty big rats. Bigger than average rats. They were pretty big rats and they...

speaker: Albert Justice
words: They were... Well, they just...

speaker: Albert Justice
words: They liked to carry things, you know. Some people call them trade rats but I don't think they... I don't think they trade but they just carry things away.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Trade rats?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Anything that's... They don't see very good, you know. Their eyesight is not very good but they always prowl around at night, you know. And they're always looking for something but they don't... Like I say, they don't see very good but they can see something kind of shiny. They can see that now like... Or maybe a teaspoon or anything like that. It happened to be laying, you know, if you had your teaspoons all put on the table like at night ready for the next morning. Well, you go along there and maybe there'd be a couple of teaspoons missing or maybe a fork or something like that. Anything they could... Well, that's kind of shiny. They can see that at night, see. Well, they'd pack it all. Don't tell them where they'd carry it. They might carry it outside, maybe anything. But I've known them to take their old eggs.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: But I don't think they... Some people say they'll take something away from here and bring something else back. But I don't think that's true. I've never... They just take something and take it... They might take it any place. They don't take it to their nest. They don't seem to have a nest but they'll just take it anyway. Maybe they'll pick up a teaspoon here and take it over behind the desk there, maybe in another room or any place that they happen to... But them pack rats and they stink like the very dickens. You can smell them when they... They'll come in from outside, see, and then they'll get... Maybe like in your storeroom, maybe behind sacks or something or cans or boxes. They'll get down in behind there, you know, see. And you can smell them. They got the darnedest odor. Well, the only thing you can do is get them out of there. But you can't trap them. They are too short. They just... I know we've tried to trap them things time and time again but they are just too smart. They'll go right around that trap, you know, just like that. Scares my fingers. Hey, it's worth it. Well, gosh, thanks. Brand new bread here. Say, we're getting... We're getting good treatment.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what about big game?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have trouble with...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: with bear or with other big animals getting into the storeroom?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, actually not any trouble to speak of. There used to be a lot of them come down around the camps there because that is... Oh, quite recently they were... But they didn't really give us any. The only time I ever had was when I was up in Medellin Falls there. I worked up there for Ohio Match, sir.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: We were burning wood in the stove. We had a big wood range there. And they had a deal fixed right in the wall just away from the stove a little way. They had a thing fixed in there where the... what they call the blow -cook, he used to... He'd put the wood in there, see, from the outside and then it would kind of fall in so that I could reach it from the inside, see. And then there was a big lid kind of on that thing out there so that if it were raining or something, he could close that lid and the wood wouldn't get wet. But he'd keep that full of wood, see, and I could reach right there and get the wood and put it in the stove. But anyway, I don't know, one time he'd forgot to close that lid, I guess, or something. Somehow it got open. But anyway, in the evening I'd get my... like my bacon, you know, I'd have it... And I'd lay the bacon out in this big pan, see, and I'd have it over there on the table ready for the next morning. We always cooked our bacon in the oven, see. Well, I had that bacon sitting over there on that table there and it was kind of on one end of that... where the wood box was, see, and the table was... And that darn lid was open out there somewhere or other and this bear got... He smelled that bacon, of course, you know, they can smell that from... And he smelled that bacon, see, and it was right close to that darn wood box there. Well, I slept just off the kitchen, just like... And I woke up there at night and I heard the rocket out there. I couldn't imagine, you know, I heard them banging around out there and... And I laid there a minute and I thought, oh my God, somebody's trying to... So I got up and I turned on the light and that bear, he was just about halfway in that... He was just about halfway in that... he just had... he paused. He got through that wood, see, and he'd kind of... He'd kind of kicked it to one side so he could get in under that and he'd smelled that bacon there. And just about... he was about half in there. And of course, when I turned on the light, well, he smelled me and he took off. But that was... I never really... but then... One of the bears there, one night he broke into the... We had a big root house out there, you know, what they called a root house, kind of dug down into the... and sided up with logs and then had a roof on it. The roof wasn't very high off the ground, you know, you could... And then they had a kind of a cupola on it, you know, to kind of let for ventilation. See, we kept our vegetables and a lot of stuff in there. So by golly, you know, son of a gun, he... Them things are powerful. He tore that cupola off of that... off of that root house and it was a big strong thing made there, you know. And he tore that darn cupola off of there and then he dropped down into the root house, down into where that stuff was. Well then, of course, he couldn't get out.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, he was in there the next morning.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well, I didn't know nothing about that, so one of the flunkies that went out there was... He used to go out there to get the potatoes, you know, he went out to peel some potatoes. He was going to get the potatoes out of the root house and bring them up to the kitchen. He went out... went down there to get them potatoes and here that bear was in there.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Well... The bear come tearing out of there, I guess he was more scared than...

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Hmm... Well, I never had you had in trouble, though.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: I just thought of one more thing I didn't ask you. It's silly because we could just keep going on and on. Well, I wouldn't say a lot of them, but... They'd haul a garbage hole, you know, and be... Oh, quite a waste in the camp, maybe a quarter of a mile, something like that. And what they'd do, they'd take the bulldozer and they'd gouge out a great big hole, you know. Great big hole, maybe as big as this room, you know, see, for all the garbage and everything. Well, then every day they'd haul the garbage and dump it in that big hole. Well, of course, there was all kinds of things in there, bones and everything. Bears would come there, you know, and scavenge on that... On that sloppo. Well, that's what used to draw them, you know, they'd know that stuff from... Come to the sloppo.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: But they did one terrible thing there one night, I never would forgive him for that. It was just Mel Carandus and Jack... Axel's brother, he was running camp there. And there was a big tree right close to camp, pretty big high tree there right outside of that. And this little bear, he would come down there, kind of scavenging around the camp there. So, some of the guys went out there and they kind of scared him and he went up that tree, see. Pretty high. No, he was a small, I don't think he weighed over 150 pounds.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Anyway... He was drinking, see. And Mel Carandus, that was Axel's brother, he was a terrible drinker, awful drinker. He was a real good guy. A real fine fellow and everything. And he was camp for them and then he'd been drinking, he was kind of drunk. Ah, they saw that bear up that tree there, see. A bunch of them got around there and they was all kind of happy. So, what they decided to do was saw the tree down, see. And then the other, I went well in one of the... Well, I hated to see that because a bear wasn't hurting anything and I knew that he'd never survived that tree coming down that way. So, they did. They sawed the tree down and fell way off across, there was a little creek down there. Fell way over across the... And of course the bear, he was way up, putting her up the top there. Well, when the tree fell, it broke his back, I guess, see.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Did the spry go much?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: No, they all ran over there to see them. Well, the bear, he couldn't run or nothing. So, they had to kill the bear. Well, I thought that was the kind of... I didn't like that. But they were all drunk, you know, and they were having a big time. They were going to get that bear. Well, I thought that was the kind of unnecessary thing to do. Well, that's the way, you get a bunch of guys like that.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Thought they didn't hurt anything. They were no problem.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: I wanted to ask you what the kitchen crew wages were. I forgot to ask you that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wages?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Yeah, what the guys working in the kitchen made.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they on monthly?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: No, they were on monthly. Food industry, I'll tell you, has always been a low paid industry. Even the hotels in town here, I think they're the poorest paid class of people compared with other workers like cartoners and all of that. But, well, the wages in the kitchen was like, goody like the food. They kept, of course, the wages kept going up and up and up. But, when I first started, I think I worked for thirty dollars a month in the camp. And, that was seven days a week, remember, every day of the week, every day of the month.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that as cook or as kitchen help?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: That was just a flunk of kitchen help or a dishwasher, whatever, you know. And, out of that thirty dollars, they took one dollar out for what they used to call a hospital fee, you know. That is, if you got sick or something like, in most of those big companies they had their company hospital, you know. Well, if you got, that is, real sick or something, they'd take you to the hospital and it didn't cost anything. But, they did take a dollar a month out of your wages every month. And, that was all that came out of it them days. There was no, but anyway, then of course the wages kept coming up and up and up. The cooks there, they kept, oh they, I think when I first bought my first job, I think I got about a hundred and twenty -five dollars a month in there, as I can remember, you know. I think that's about what I was making, about a hundred and twenty -five a month, of course. We got all our, there was nothing come out of that like I say, only maybe there was no, in no social security or anything.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: But, I think that Molly and May came to work there in about, what was the year you came to work there, twenty -six or? Twenty -four, twenty, at old camp seven there, how much were you getting? I was getting about forty dollars a month, wasn't you?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Sixty, May said. No, that was twenty, twenty -two.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Huh?

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: That was twenty -two.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Was it?

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Why sure. Yeah, I guess that's right. First time I see you guys, you and your brother. Well, wages kept coming up and they got up to be forty, forty -five dollars a month. And then of course they kept coming up, quick wages kept going up. And I think when I left, well I see there, I think there was about three hundred and forty a month for, you know, monthly wages for the cooks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How about the, did the baker, was the baker a higher paid?

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Oh no, no the baker, I forget now, I don't know what Ralph was getting there, exactly, but oh he was probably getting about three hundred. I can't just remember. And then of course the meat cutter and the second cook and the whole family.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: But the miscellaneous help, I tell you, they, I can remember when they worked for forty, forty -five dollars a month and got up to sixty dollars a month.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Oh I don't know, I guess the cooks now in camp, what view there is, they're making around six hundred a month.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: About to die.

speaker: Albert Justice
words: Of course, it was just changing times and just like now, wages keep going up and up and up.
